Because amorphous solids are disordered and "glassy," they do not have a characteristic heat of sublimation, as the energy needed to release particles from their random positions is not constant.

This is the correct option because all the statements are scientifically accurate. The lack of a repeating internal pattern (crystal lattice) directly results in the absence of sharp melting points and specific, constant values for heats of fusion or sublimation.

questions.explanation: Amorphous solids are fundamentally different from crystalline solids because they do not have a regular, repeating arrangement of atoms or molecules. In a crystal, every bond is identical, so they all break at the same temperature, giving it a sharp melting point and a specific heat of fusion. However, in an amorphous solid like glass or plastic, the particles are "frozen" in a messy, random way, with some bonds being stronger or weaker than others. Consequently, when heat is applied, the weaker bonds break first, and the material slowly softens into a thick liquid over a wide temperature range, instead of melting instantly. Because there is no fixed temperature or uniform structure for these phase changes, these substances do not possess characteristic or constant values for their heat of fusion or sublimation.

questions.text: Amorphous substances do not have
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Amorphous substances lack an ordered, repeating internal structure. Because their molecules are arranged randomly, their thermal properties (like how heat flows or how the material expands) can be uneven compared to a perfect crystal, though they are isotropic overall. They do not have the uniform "grid-like" response to heat that crystalline solids do.

Amorphous solids do not have a sharp boiling point (or melting point). Instead of changing phase at one exact temperature, they gradually soften and turn into a liquid over a wide temperature range because the bonds in their disordered structure vary in strength and break at different times.

Enthalpy of fusion or sublimation refers to a fixed amount of energy needed to change the state of a substance. Since amorphous solids are essentially "frozen" liquids with no fixed arrangement, the energy required to break their bonds is inconsistent, meaning they do not possess a definite or characteristic enthalpy for phase changes.

This is the correct choice because the lack of a regular crystal lattice in amorphous materials (like glass or plastic) means they lack all the fixed, sharp, and definite physical constants that characterize crystalline solids.

questions.explanation: The primary way we distinguish between crystalline and amorphous solids is through their physical constants. In a true crystalline solid, every atom is in a perfect row, meaning every bond is identical and requires the same amount of energy to break; this results in a sharp melting point and a definite enthalpy. However, amorphous substances are basically "disordered" at the molecular level, looking more like a snapshot of a liquid than a structured solid. Because some molecules are closer together and others are further apart, there is no single temperature where the whole substance changes state, nor is there a specific, constant amount of heat energy (enthalpy) associated with its phase transitions. Therefore, these substances do not show uniform thermal behavior, sharp boiling/melting points, or definite enthalpies of fusion.

questions.text: Which one doesn't have uniform thermodynamic properties?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Crystalline solids have a highly ordered and repeating three-dimensional arrangement of particles. Because every part of the crystal is identical and every bond is of the same strength, crystalline solids possess uniform and definite thermodynamic properties, such as a sharp melting point and a specific heat of fusion.

Amorphous solids lack a regular repeating structure, which means the distances and forces between their particles are not constant. This lack of uniformity at the molecular level results in non-uniform thermodynamic properties; for instance, they do not have a sharp melting point, and their heat of fusion is not a fixed value.

Liquids are also characterized by a disordered arrangement of particles where molecules are constantly moving and sliding past each other. This lack of fixed long-range order means that, like amorphous solids, liquids do not have the definite and sharp thermodynamic constants (like specific enthalpies for internal structural changes) that organized crystals do.

This is the correct choice because both amorphous solids and liquids are disordered states of matter. Unlike crystalline solids, which have a "perfect" grid that behaves predictably, the random nature of both liquids and amorphous solids prevents them from having uniform or definite thermodynamic properties.

questions.explanation: Thermodynamic properties like melting point, heat of fusion, and enthalpy of sublimation are considered "uniform" when they occur at a single, precise value. In a crystalline solid, the internal structure is like a perfectly organized brick wall where every brick is held by the same amount of cement; therefore, the whole wall "breaks" (melts) at once when a specific amount of heat is reached. However, amorphous solids and liquids are disordered, more like a messy pile of bricks where some are held tightly, and others are loose. Because of this internal "messiness," there is no single temperature or energy value that applies to the entire substance at once. Amorphous solids soften over a wide range of temperatures, and liquids possess a statistical distribution of energies rather than one fixed structural energy, which is why both categories are considered to have non-uniform thermodynamic properties compared to crystals.

questions.text: Pseudo solid substance have
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: seudo solids (also known as amorphous solids or supercooled liquids) lack a regular, long-range repeating pattern of particles. Because their internal structure is disordered and inconsistent, they do not possess uniform thermodynamic properties; for example, they soften over a range of temperatures instead of having a sharp melting point.

Pseudo solids do not inherently have greater tensile strength compared to crystalline solids. In fact, many pseudo solids like glass are quite brittle, and their strength depends heavily on their specific composition and how they were cooled, rather than a superior structural arrangement.

Most pseudo solids, such as glass, rubber, and plastics, are actually excellent insulators and do not show electrical conductivity. Conductivity typically requires a sea of delocalized electrons (as in metals) or mobile ions (as in molten salts), neither of which are characteristic of common pseudo solids.

This is the correct choice because none of the listed positive traits (A, B, or C) are defining characteristics of pseudo solids. Pseudo solids are defined by their lack of order, their tendency to flow very slowly over time, and their isotropic nature, rather than uniform thermodynamics or high conductivity.

questions.explanation: Pseudo solids are substances like glass, rubber, and pitch that appear to be solid because they are rigid and have a definite shape, but they actually lack the organized crystal lattice of a "true" solid. Because they are essentially liquids that have been cooled so quickly that their molecules are "frozen" in a messy, random arrangement, they do not have the definite physical constants found in crystals. This means they do not have uniform thermodynamic properties like a sharp melting point, and they generally do not conduct electricity or possess unusually high tensile strength. Instead of behaving like a perfect geometric grid, a pseudo solid behaves like a very thick, non-flowing liquid, which is why they are often called supercooled liquids.

questions.text: Mr. Khan studies a solid crystalline substance & the isotropic properties he founds is
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Reactivity is a chemical property and does not depend on the isotropy or anisotropy of a material.
Refractive index is a physical property that can vary in different directions in anisotropic materials, hence it is not isotropic.
Optical properties, such as birefringence, can exhibit anisotropy in crystalline substances.
Thermal conductivity can vary with direction in crystalline solids, so it is not an isotropic property.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Reactivity. Isotropic properties are those that are the same in all directions. In crystalline solids, physical properties like refractive index and thermal conductivity can be anisotropic, meaning they differ based on direction. However, chemical properties such as reactivity are not dependent on direction and are considered isotropic in nature. This is why reactivity is the correct answer, while the other options are incorrect due to their potential anisotropy in crystalline solids.

questions.text: The two compounds A and B are isomorphs because
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Isomorphs are compounds that share the same crystal structure due to similarities in their lattice arrangement.
While similar chemical formulas may lead to isomorphism, the defining characteristic of isomorphs is their similar crystal structure.

options.text: They are both soluble in water
options.explanation: Solubility in water is not a criterion for isomorphism, which is determined by crystal structure.
Identical melting points can occur for various reasons and do not necessarily indicate isomorphism.

questions.explanation: Isomorphs are compounds that have the same crystal structure and lattice arrangement. Option A is correct because isomorphs share a similar crystal structure, which is the key characteristic of isomorphism. Option B is incorrect because isomorphism is not defined by chemical formula alone. Option C is incorrect as solubility is not related to isomorphism. Option D is incorrect because identical melting points are not a defining factor for isomorphism.

questions.text: Select a pair of compounds that are isomorphic in nature.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: NaCl has a rock salt (cubic) structure, whereas ZnO usually has a hexagonal structure. Hence, they are not isomorphic.
Both NaCl and MgO crystallize in the rock salt (cubic) structure. Because they have the same crystal form, they are isomorphic.
KNO3 has a different crystal structure compared to MgO, so they are not isomorphic.
ZnO and CaCO₃ have different crystal systems, therefore they are not isomorphic.

questions.explanation: Isomorphic compounds have the same crystal structure. Since both NaCl and MgO possess the rock salt structure, they are isomorphic in nature.

questions.text: Isomorphic substances have
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Incorrect. If both physical and chemical properties were the same, the substances would essentially be identical, not just isomorphic.

Correct. Isomorphic substances have the same crystal structure, so many physical properties (like shape, symmetry) are similar, but their chemical composition is different, so chemical properties differ.
Incorrect. Same chemical properties with different physical ones usually relate to polymorphism, not isomorphism.
Incorrect. This does not describe isomorphism.

questions.explanation: Isomorphic substances possess the same crystal structure, leading to similar physical properties, but since their chemical compositions differ, their chemical properties are different.

questions.text: The polymorphism of an element is called
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Isomorphism refers to the similarity in the crystal structure of different compounds and not the polymorphism of an element.
Allotropy is the correct term for the phenomenon where an element exists in two or more different forms in the same physical state.
Anisotropy describes the directional dependence of a material's properties, not the polymorphism of an element.
Isotropy refers to the uniformity in all directions of a material's properties, not polymorphism.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Allotropy. Allotropy is the phenomenon where an element can exist in two or more different forms in the same physical state. These different forms are known as allotropes, and they often have distinct physical properties and structures. For instance, carbon can exist as diamond, graphite, and graphene, which are all allotropes. The term Isomorphism is used to describe compounds with similar crystal structures, not elements. Anisotropy refers to directional dependence of properties, and Isotropy refers to uniformity in properties, neither of which relate to the concept of polymorphism in elements.

questions.text: Which of the following has a hexagonal structure?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Diamond has a cubic crystal structure, not hexagonal.
Sodium chloride forms a cubic crystal lattice, not hexagonal.
Graphite has a hexagonal crystal structure, which consists of layers of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal pattern.
Neither diamond nor sodium chloride have hexagonal structures; both have cubic structures.

questions.explanation: Graphite has a hexagonal crystal structure. It is composed of layers where each carbon atom is bonded to three others in a plane, creating a pattern of hexagons. In contrast, diamond has a cubic crystal structure where each carbon atom is tetrahedrally coordinated. Sodium chloride also forms a cubic crystal lattice, specifically a face-centered cubic structure. Thus, only graphite has the hexagonal arrangement.

questions.text: Among the following unit cells given below, which one has the highest degree of symmetry?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Correct. The cubic unit cell has maximum symmetry. All edges are equal (a = b = c) and all angles are 90°, giving it the highest degree of symmetry among crystal systems.
Monoclinic unit cells have least symmetry, with unequal edges and only two angles at 90°.
Hexagonal systems have moderate symmetry, but less than cubic.
Orthorhombic systems also have lower symmetry than cubic, with unequal edges though angles are 90°.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the cubic unit cell. It exhibits the highest degree of symmetry due to its equal edge lengths and all internal angles being 90°. This geometric uniformity allows for multiple symmetrical planes, making it the most symmetrical of the unit cell types listed. In contrast, the monoclinic unit cell has only one 90° angle and differing edge lengths, leading to fewer symmetrical planes. The hexagonal unit cell, although having some symmetry, does not match the cubic's symmetry due to its 120° angle. Lastly, the orthorhombic unit cell has three unequal edge lengths, resulting in less symmetry than the cubic cell.

questions.text: Which of the following is true solid?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Gemstones are crystalline solids that have a definite geometric structure and fixed shape.
Glass is an amorphous solid, meaning it lacks a long-range order that characterizes true solids.
Rubber is an amorphous solid with a flexible structure, not a true solid.
Plastic is an amorphous or semi-crystalline solid that does not have the definite structure of a true solid.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Gemstone. True solids are characterized by a definite geometric structure and long-range order, which gemstones possess due to their crystalline nature. In contrast, glass, rubber, and plastic are considered amorphous solids, lacking this long-range order and definite structure.

questions.text: When a crystal is rotated along it axis by 360°, the repetition of angles, faces, and edges is called
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: A unit cell is the smallest repeating building block of a crystal lattice. While the unit cell defines the shape and symmetry of the crystal, it refers to the physical structure itself rather than the mathematical repetition of features during a rotation.

The center of symmetry is a specific point in a crystal such that any line drawn through it will find identical points at equal distances on opposite sides. It is a static element of symmetry (inversion) rather than the process of repeating faces or edges through rotation.

This is the correct option. Symmetry (specifically rotational symmetry) describes the phenomenon where a crystal looks identical several times during a 360-degree rotation. When faces, angles, and edges repeat themselves, it confirms that the crystal possesses symmetry elements that define its geometric class.

Geometry is a broad branch of mathematics that deals with shapes, sizes, and relative positions of figures. While the geometry of a crystal determines its symmetry, the specific term for the "repetition" observed during rotation is symmetry.

questions.explanation: Crystal symmetry is the regular repetition of similar faces, edges, and angles when a crystal is rotated around an axis. This behavior is a direct result of the ordered internal arrangement of atoms within the crystal lattice. If a crystal is rotated 360 degrees and its appearance remains unchanged four times (every 90 degrees), we say it has a "four-fold" axis of symmetry. These repeating features are used by scientists to classify crystals into different systems and classes, ensuring that the external beauty of a crystal is a perfect reflection of its internal mathematical order.

questions.text: A unit cell can have only one
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: A plane of symmetry is an imaginary plane that divides a crystal into two identical halves that are mirror images of each other. Depending on its geometry, a unit cell can have multiple planes; for example, a cubic unit cell possesses nine different planes of symmetry.

An axis of symmetry is an imaginary line around which the crystal can be rotated to repeat its appearance. Most symmetrical crystal systems have several such axes; a cube, for instance, has thirteen axes of symmetry, including 2-fold, 3-fold, and 4-fold axes.

This is the correct option. The centre of symmetry is a single point at the exact geometric center of the unit cell. By definition, a body can only have one unique central point such that any line drawn through it meets identical points at equal distances on opposite sides.

This option is incorrect because, as shown above, crystals often possess numerous planes and axes of symmetry. Only the centre of symmetry is limited to a single occurrence within any individual unit cell.

questions.explanation: In crystallography, symmetry elements help us define the regularity of a crystal. While a complex shape like a cube can be cut by many mirror planes or spun around many different axes to look the same, it can only have one true heart or center. This single "Centre of Symmetry" is the specific point where every part of the crystal has a perfect opposite partner across the middle. Because a geometric shape can only have one mathematical center, a unit cell can never have more than one centre of symmetry, even if it has dozens of planes and axes.

questions.text: Crystal growth is effected by
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The method of preparation determines how the crystal lattice is formed, affecting crystal growth.
Conditions such as temperature and pressure during preparation can significantly influence crystal growth.
Impurities can disrupt the crystal lattice, affecting the size and quality of the crystals that form.
All these factors collectively impact the growth of crystals. The method, conditions, and presence of impurities all play critical roles.

questions.explanation: Crystal growth is affected by a combination of factors. The method of preparation determines the foundational structure of the crystals. The conditions of preparation, such as temperature, pressure, and saturation, can influence the rate and quality of growth. Impurities can interfere with the orderly arrangement of atoms or molecules, leading to defects or variations in the crystal. Therefore, All of the above is the correct answer, as all these factors influence crystal growth in different ways.

questions.text: When 10% urea is present in the saturated solution of NaCl. Then the crystal grows in
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: One-dimensional growth leads to the formation of needles or whiskers. While some impurities can cause this, 10% urea specifically modifies the habit (external shape) of the salt crystal by interacting with its surfaces in all directions, rather than restricting it to a single line of growth.

Two-dimensional growth results in thin plates or flakes. While urea does block certain faces of the crystal, it does not stop the crystal from expanding into a solid volume. The urea allows the crystal to maintain its bulk form while simply changing the orientation of its faces.

This is the correct option. Even with 10% urea, the NaCl crystal still grows as a three-dimensional solid object. The urea acts as an "impurity" that sticks to the {111} faces of the crystal, slowing their growth and changing the shape from a standard cube to an octahedron (an 8-sided 3D shape).

This option is incorrect because the effect of urea on NaCl is a classic and well-predicted experiment in chemistry. Scientists know exactly how the urea molecules interfere with the salt ions to create the octahedral 3D habit.

questions.explanation: Sodium chloride normally grows as a cube because its particles stack equally in three dimensions. When 10% urea is added to a saturated solution, the urea molecules act as "surface blockers" that preferentially stick to the diagonal faces of the growing salt crystal. This slows down the growth in those specific directions, causing the crystal to develop an octahedral (eight-faced) shape instead of a cube. Despite this change in "habit" or external look, the crystal remains a three-dimensional object. This experiment proves that external impurities can change the geometric shape of a crystal without changing its internal cubic lattice structure.

questions.text: The differences of physical properties in different directions for a given crystal is called
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Isotropy refers to the property of a substance where its physical properties are identical in all directions. This is characteristic of amorphous solids (like glass) and liquids because their particles are arranged randomly, making the "average" environment the same no matter which direction you measure.

Isomorphism is a structural term describing different chemical compounds (like NaCl and MgO) that share the same crystal shape. It deals with the similarity of geometry between two different substances, not the directional dependence of properties within a single crystal.

This is the correct option. Anisotropy is the variation of physical properties (such as refractive index, thermal conductivity, or electrical resistance) based on the direction in which they are measured. It occurs because particles in a crystal are packed differently along different axes.

Allotropy describes the existence of a single element in more than one physical form (like diamond and graphite for carbon). While allotropes themselves are often anisotropic, the term for the directional difference in properties is anisotropy.

questions.explanation: Anisotropy is a defining feature of crystalline solids because of their highly ordered internal structure. In a crystal, atoms are arranged in specific rows and layers; the distance between these atoms and the strength of the bonds can change depending on the path you take through the lattice. For example, in graphite, electrons flow easily along the layers but struggle to jump between layers, making its electrical conductivity different in different directions. This "directional dependence" is what we call anisotropy, whereas amorphous solids are isotropic because their random messiness looks the same from every angle.

questions.text: Crystalline solids are anisotropic because of
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Crystalline solids are anisotropic because they exhibit different properties in different directions due to their ordered structure.
While crystalline solids do have a high order of particles, this alone does not contribute to anisotropy. It is the specific directional order that matters.
High density is a general property of solids but does not explain the directional variation in properties.
A high melting point is a characteristic of many solids due to strong intermolecular forces, but it is unrelated to anisotropy.

questions.explanation: Crystalline solids are anisotropic because their properties vary with direction. This anisotropy is due to the uniform and repetitive arrangement of particles in specific directions within the crystal lattice. The correct answer is the 'Directional dependence of properties', as it directly addresses the anisotropic nature caused by the crystal structure.Option B, 'High order of particles,' is incorrect because while the ordered structure is a feature, it is the directional nature of this order that causes anisotropy. Option C, 'High density,' is incorrect as density does not affect directional properties. Option D, 'High melting point,' is incorrect because the melting point is related to bond strength, not anisotropy.

questions.text: Which of the following pair shows isomorphic substances?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: ZnO and CdS have similar Hexagonal crystal structures, making them isomorphic.
KNO3 (Orthorhombic) has a different crystal structure compared to CdS (hexagonal), so they are not isomorphic.
While MgO (Cubic) and ZnO (hexagonal) are both oxides, they do not share the same crystal structure, so they are not isomorphic.
NaCl has a cubic structure, which differs from CdS (Hexagonal); therefore, they are not isomorphic.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is ZnO & CdS because both substances crystallize in similar hexagonal wurtzite structures, making them isomorphic. Isomorphic substances have similar crystal structures but can have different chemical compositions.KNO₃ and CdS are not isomorphic because KNO₃ has a trigonal crystal system, whereas CdS has a hexagonal structure. MgO & ZnO are not isomorphic because MgO typically crystallizes in a cubic form, unlike ZnO. Finally, NaCl & CdS are not isomorphic because NaCl has a cubic crystal structure, which is different from the hexagonal structure of CdS.

questions.text: The crystalline forms of a substance which coexist at transition temperature is called
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Polymorphs are different crystalline forms of the same chemical compound. For many substances, there is a specific temperature where two different crystal structures are both equally stable and can exist together in equilibrium. This phenomenon is a general feature of polymorphic substances when they transition from one shape to another.

Allotropes are the different crystalline forms of a single element (like rhombic and monoclinic sulfur). Since allotropy is essentially the "polymorphism of elements," these forms also coexist in equilibrium at a specific transition temperature. For example, at 95.6°C, both rhombic and monoclinic sulfur exist simultaneously.

Isomorphs are different chemical substances (like NaCl and MgO) that share the same crystal shape. Because they are entirely different chemicals, they do not "coexist" or transform into one another at a transition temperature. They remain separate entities with their own unique melting points.

This is the correct option because the term "substance" includes both elements and compounds. Transition temperature is defined as the temperature at which two crystalline forms of a substance—whether they are called polymorphs (for compounds) or allotropes (for elements)—can coexist in equilibrium.

questions.explanation: The transition temperature is a unique point where two different crystalline arrangements of the same substance are in perfect balance. At this exact temperature, the vapor pressures of both forms are equal, allowing them to live side-by-side without one turning into the other. This applies to elements, where the forms are called allotropes (like sulfur), and to compounds, where the forms are called polymorphs (like KNO3). Because both terms describe the same physical reality of multiple forms existing for one substance, both are correct in this context.

questions.text: The most common allotropic forms of Sulphur S8 are
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The most common allotropic forms of sulfur are rhombic (α-sulfur) and monoclinic (β-sulfur), both of which consist of S8 molecules.

Liquid and gaseous are states of matter, not allotropic forms. While sulfur can melt into a liquid or boil into a gas, these terms describe how much energy the molecules have rather than how the atoms are structurally arranged in a solid crystal lattice.

Amorphous and crystalline are broad categories of solids. While sulfur has an amorphous form (plastic sulfur) and multiple crystalline forms (rhombic, monoclinic), this option is too general. The question specifically asks for the most common "forms" of the S8 molecule, which are the two specific crystals listed in Option A.

Cubic and tetragonal are crystal systems used to classify many different minerals. While some forms of sulfur can be forced into different systems under high pressure, the common natural allotropes of S8 follow the orthorhombic (for rhombic) and monoclinic systems specifically.

questions.explanation: Sulfur is famous for having more allotropes than almost any other element, but the two most important ones are rhombic and monoclinic sulfur. Both of these forms are made of the exact same S8 molecules, which look like tiny "puckered" crowns. The only difference is how these crowns are stacked together: rhombic sulfur forms chunky, octahedral crystals that are stable in cool conditions, while monoclinic sulfur forms long, thin needle-like crystals when heated above the transition temperature of 95.6°C. Because they share the same molecular formula but have different crystal shapes, they are perfect examples of allotropy in elements.

questions.text: The allotropic forms of sulphur (S8) are
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Sulfur does not commonly exist in a cubic form. While it can be forced into different crystal systems under extremely high-pressure conditions, the standard S8 rings naturally arrange themselves into orthorhombic or monoclinic lattices. Cubic structures are more typical for metals and simple salts like NaCl.

The tetragonal system is not a standard allotropic form for the S8 molecule. Although sulfur has many rare allotropes (nearly 30 are known), the two dominant crystalline forms are defined by their orthorhombic (rhombic) and monoclinic symmetries.

This is the correct option. Rhombic sulfur (⍺ sulfur) and Monoclinic sulfur (β sulfur) are the two most important crystalline allotropes of sulfur. Both are composed of S8 puckered rings (crown shape), but they differ in how these rings are packed together, which changes their crystal system and stability.

While rhombic sulfur is a primary allotrope, sulfur does not naturally form a stable hexagonal structure in its S8 molecular state. Hexagonal structures are found in other elements like graphite or certain metal oxides like ZnO, but not in common elemental sulfur.

questions.explanation: Sulfur exists in different structural forms called allotropes, with rhombic and monoclinic being the most common crystalline varieties. Both forms are made of the same S8 molecules, which look like tiny crowns. Rhombic sulfur is the stable form at room temperature and has chunky, octahedral crystals. When you heat it above 95.6°C (the transition temperature), it turns into monoclinic sulfur, which forms long, needle-like crystals. These two forms can coexist at that specific transition temperature, showing how one element can have different physical shapes based on how its molecules are arranged.

questions.text: Which of the following have different physical properties?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Polymorphs are different crystalline forms of the same chemical compound (like CaCO3 as calcite or aragonite). Because the atoms are arranged in different geometric patterns, they have different physical properties such as hardness, density, and melting points, even though they are made of the same chemical.

Isomorphs are different chemical substances that share the same crystal structure (like NaF and MgO). Since they are composed of different elements and ions, they have different physical properties like mass, color, and boiling points, despite their identical external appearance.

Allotropes are the different forms of a single element (like diamond and graphite for carbon). These forms exhibit vastly different physical properties; for example, diamond is the hardest natural substance and an insulator, while graphite is soft, slippery, and conducts electricity.

This is the correct option because all three categories—polymorphs, isomorphs, and allotropes—involve substances that possess distinct physical characteristics. Whether the difference comes from a change in atomic arrangement (polymorphism/allotropy) or a difference in chemical identity (isomorphism), the resulting physical properties are never identical.

questions.explanation: In crystalline chemistry, the way atoms are arranged or the identity of the atoms themselves determines the physical behavior of a solid. Polymorphs and allotropes show different properties because their internal "building plan" changes, even if the "material" (the element or compound) is the same. Isomorphs show different properties because the "material" is different, even if the "building plan" (the crystal shape) is the same. Therefore, in all these cases, you will find variations in characteristics like density, hardness, and conductivity, making "All of these" the right choice.

questions.text: Crystalline substances that exist at the transition temperature have
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Crystalline substances at transition temperature have consistent chemical properties because the chemical nature remains unchanged during phase transitions.
This is incorrect because during transition, physical properties such as structure and state can change.
This is incorrect as transition temperatures can lead to changes in unit cell dimensions due to structural rearrangements.
This is incorrect because, although chemical properties remain the same, physical properties can change during a transition.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that crystalline substances at the transition temperature have the same chemical properties. The chemical properties of a substance are determined by its molecular composition, which does not change during a phase transition. However, physical properties such as shape, volume, and structure can change during a transition. Therefore, option A is correct, while options B, C, and D are incorrect because they suggest that physical properties remain unchanged, which is not the case during a phase transition.

questions.text: Quartz (SiO2) and cristobalite (SiO2) are
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements at the molecular level. Quartz and cristobalite have the same chemical formula (SiO₂) but differ in crystal structure, not molecular structure.

Isomorphs are compounds that have a similar crystal structure but different chemical compositions. Quartz and cristobalite have the same chemical composition (SiO₂) but different crystal structures, making this option incorrect.
Allotropes are different forms of the same element in the same physical state. Quartz and cristobalite are different structural forms of SiO₂, not of an elemental form, so this option is incorrect.
Polymorphs are different crystal forms of the same chemical compound. Quartz and cristobalite are both SiO₂ but have different crystal structures, making them polymorphs.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Polymorphs. Quartz and cristobalite are both polymorphs of silica (SiO₂). This means they have the same chemical composition but different crystal structures. Quartz has a trigonal crystal system, while cristobalite has a tetragonal crystal system. This difference in structure but not in chemical composition defines polymorphism.The other options are incorrect: Isomers pertain to molecular compounds with the same molecular formula but different arrangements of atoms, which doesn't apply to mineral structures. Isomorphs refer to different compounds with similar crystal structures, which is not the case here. Allotropes are different forms of the same element, not a compound like SiO₂.

questions.text: Lattice energy of an ionic compound depends upon
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The packing of ions contributes to the stability of the crystal structure but is not a direct factor in determining lattice energy.
The size of the ion affects lattice energy, but it is not the sole factor. Both size and charge must be considered.

options.text: Charge and size of the ion
options.explanation: Lattice energy is influenced by both the charge and size of the ions. Higher charges and smaller ionic radii result in greater lattice energy.
While the charge on the ion is crucial, lattice energy is also dependent on the ionic size.

questions.explanation: Lattice energy is a measure of the strength of the bonds in an ionic solid, and it depends on both the charge and size of the ions involved. Generally, ions with higher charges and smaller sizes will have higher lattice energies because the electrostatic forces between them are stronger. Option A is incorrect because the packing of ions affects crystal stability, not lattice energy directly. Option B is incorrect because size alone doesn't determine lattice energy; charge must be considered. Option D is incorrect because charge alone doesn't account for the effects of ionic size.

questions.text: Which of the following have covalent crystal?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Blue vitriol, or copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate, forms an ionic crystal structure, not a covalent one.
Silicon forms a giant covalent crystal lattice, similar to that of diamond, making it a covalent crystal.
Ice forms a molecular crystal where water molecules are held together by hydrogen bonds, not covalent bonds.
Copper forms a metallic crystal lattice, characterized by metallic bonds, rather than covalent bonds.

questions.explanation: Silicon is the correct answer because it forms a giant covalent structure, where each silicon atom is covalently bonded to four other silicon atoms in a tetrahedral arrangement, similar to diamond. This structure is characteristic of covalent crystals. In contrast, Blue vitriol (copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate) is an ionic compound, ice is a molecular crystal with hydrogen bonding, and copper forms metallic crystals with metallic bonding.

questions.text: Which of the following force is responsible for the existence of iodine in the solid form at normal condition?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Dipole-dipole forces occur between polar molecules. Iodine is a nonpolar molecule, so this force is not responsible for its solid state.
London dispersion forces are weak intermolecular forces that arise from temporary dipoles in atoms or molecules. They are responsible for the solid state of iodine under normal conditions.

Dipole-induced dipole forces occur when a polar molecule induces a dipole in a nonpolar molecule. Since iodine is nonpolar and does not interact with polar molecules in this context, this is not the correct force.
Not all forces listed are responsible for the solid state of iodine. Only London dispersion forces are relevant in this case.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is London dispersion force. These forces are the only intermolecular forces that can act between nonpolar molecules like iodine (I2). Despite their weakness, they are sufficient to hold iodine molecules together in a solid form at room temperature due to the relatively large number of electrons in iodine, which enhances these forces. The other options focus on interactions that involve polar molecules, which do not apply to iodine in its natural state.

questions.text: Which of the following have no cleavage plane?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: NaCl, or sodium chloride, has a cubic crystal structure with well-defined cleavage planes due to its ionic bonds.
Ice has a hexagonal crystal structure, which allows it to cleave along certain planes, especially as it is a molecular solid held together by hydrogen bonds.
Copper is a metallic crystal and does not have cleavage planes. Instead, it can deform by slipping along planes, but does not cleave in the way ionic or covalent crystals do.
Graphite has a layered structure with weak Van der Waals forces between layers, allowing it to cleave easily along those planes.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Copper crystal. Unlike ionic compounds like NaCl or molecular solids like ice, metallic crystals, such as copper, do not have defined cleavage planes because the metal atoms are arranged in a way that allows for malleability and ductility, not cleavage. In contrast, NaCl and ice have specific structures that allow for cleavage along certain planes. Graphite, due to its layered structure, also has cleavage planes between its layers.

questions.text: Which of the following crystalline solids do not exist in liquid or gaseous form at ordinary conditions of temperature & pressure?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Metallic solids, such as iron and copper, are characterized by a lattice of metal cations surrounded by a sea of delocalized electrons. They remain solid at ordinary conditions due to strong metallic bonding.

Ionic solids, like sodium chloride, consist of a regular arrangement of ions held together by strong electrostatic forces. These solids do not exist in liquid or gaseous form at ordinary conditions because of the high energy required to overcome these forces.
Molecular solids, such as ice or dry ice, are held together by weaker intermolecular forces, allowing them to easily transition to liquid or gaseous states at relatively low temperatures.

Covalent solids, like diamond or silicon carbide, are formed by atoms connected in a large network by covalent bonds. While they are very stable and solid at ordinary conditions, the question asks for those that do not exist in liquid or gaseous form, which applies more specifically to ionic solids.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is an ionic solid. Ionic solids do not exist in liquid or gaseous form at ordinary temperatures and pressures because the strong electrostatic forces between the ions require significant energy to break. Metallic solids can exist in liquid form when melted, molecular solids can easily transition to liquids or gases due to their weak intermolecular forces, and covalent solids, while remaining stable and solid, focus on network structure rather than the state at ordinary conditions.

questions.text: Which of the following is polar molecular crystal?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Iodine molecules are non-polar due to the equal sharing of electrons between the two iodine atoms.
Sulphur forms covalent non-polar molecules, as the S8 rings have no dipole moment.
Ice is made of water molecules, which are polar due to the bent shape and difference in electronegativity between hydrogen and oxygen.
Carbon disulfide is a linear molecule with non-polar characteristics because the dipoles cancel out.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Ice. In ice, the water molecules are polar because of the bent shape and the high electronegativity of oxygen compared to hydrogen, leading to a net dipole moment.Iodine is incorrect because it forms non-polar diatomic molecules.Sulphur is incorrect because it forms non-polar S8 rings.Carbon disulfide is incorrect because it is a linear non-polar molecule, where the sulfur atoms evenly share electrons with the carbon atom, resulting in no net dipole moment.

questions.text: The compressible crystalline solid is
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Metallic solids consist of metal atoms packed closely together in a "sea of electrons." While metals are malleable (can be hammered into sheets) and ductile, they are not significantly compressible under ordinary conditions. The strong metallic bonds and close-packed atomic arrangement resist any decrease in volume.

This is the correct option. Molecular solids are composed of discrete molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces like London dispersion forces or hydrogen bonds. Because these "bridge" forces are relatively weak compared to chemical bonds, the molecules can be pushed slightly closer together under pressure, making them the most compressible crystalline solids.

Ionic solids are made of oppositely charged ions held together by very strong electrostatic forces. These ions are locked into a rigid 3D lattice with almost no empty space between them. Any attempt to compress them results in strong repulsion between like-charged ions, making them very hard and nearly incompressible.

Covalent solids (network solids) like diamond or quartz are held together by a continuous network of strong covalent bonds. These bonds are highly directional and rigid. Because the entire crystal is essentially one giant molecule, it is extremely difficult to compress, making it one of the least compressible substances known.

questions.explanation: In crystalline solids, compressibility depends on the strength of the forces holding the particles together. Molecular solids, like ice or dry ice, are made of individual molecules that are only loosely held by weak van der Waals forces. These weak forces allow the molecules to be "squeezed" closer together more easily than the atoms in metals or the ions in salts. In contrast, ionic and covalent solids have such powerful internal "glues" (ionic and covalent bonds) that their particles are already packed as tightly as possible, offering massive resistance to any external pressure.

questions.text: Which one is conductor but is not malleable
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Copper is a good conductor of electricity and is also highly malleable.
Gold is an excellent conductor and is known for its malleability, making it easily shaped into thin sheets.
Graphite is a good conductor of electricity due to the presence of free electrons, but it is brittle and not malleable.
Iron is a good conductor and is also malleable to some extent, though not as much as gold or copper.

questions.explanation: Graphite is the correct answer because it conducts electricity due to its structure of delocalized electrons, but it is not malleable as it is brittle. The other options, such as Gold, Copper, and Iron, are also conductors, but they are malleable. Gold and Copper are especially known for their ductility and malleability, while Iron, though not as malleable as Gold and Copper, still possesses some malleability.

questions.text: Malleable solid can be
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The ability of a substance to be drawn into thin wires is specifically called ductility. While most malleable materials (like gold and copper) are also ductile, the two terms describe different types of deformation. Ductility involves pulling the material (tensile stress), whereas malleability involves pressing it.

This is the correct option. Malleability is defined as the physical property of a solid, usually a metal, that allows it to be hammered, rolled, or pressed into thin sheets without cracking or breaking. This happens because the layers of atoms can slide over each other under compressive stress.

Whether a solid can be melted easily depends on its melting point, which is determined by the strength of its chemical bonds, not its malleability. For example, iron is highly malleable but has a very high melting point (1538°C), while lead is also malleable but melts at a much lower temperature (327°C).

This option is incorrect because "malleable" has a very specific definition. It strictly refers to the ability to withstand compression to form sheets. While a substance might be both malleable and ductile, or malleable with a low melting point, those are additional properties and not part of the definition of malleability itself.

questions.explanation: It is important to distinguish between different mechanical properties. Malleability comes from the nature of the metallic bond, where a "sea of electrons" allows positive metal ions to shift and relocate when they are hit with a hammer or squeezed by rollers. Because the electrons move with the ions, the bond doesn't snap; instead, the metal simply changes shape. This specific ability to flatten out into sheets (like aluminum foil or gold leaf) is what we call malleability. If the material were pulled into a wire instead, we would use the term ductility.

questions.text: Which of the following is a molecular solid?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Iodine (I2) is a non-polar molecular solid. It consists of separate I2 molecules held together in a crystal lattice by weak London dispersion forces. Because these forces are weak, iodine has a low melting point and can easily sublime (turn directly into a gas) when heated.

Wax (such as paraffin wax) is a molecular solid made of long-chain hydrocarbon molecules. These large molecules are held together by dispersion forces. This weak "intermolecular glue" is the reason why wax is soft, has a low melting point, and can be easily molded with your hands.

Ice is a polar molecular solid. Each water molecule (H2O) is a discrete unit, and the entire crystal is held together by hydrogen bonds. While hydrogen bonds are stronger than the forces in iodine or wax, they are still much weaker than the ionic or covalent bonds found in other types of solids.

This is the correct option because all three substances (Iodine, Wax, and Ice) are composed of individual, neutral molecules rather than ions or a continuous network of atoms. In all these cases, the "building blocks" are molecules held together by various types of intermolecular forces.

questions.explanation: In molecular solids like iodine, wax, and ice, the "building blocks" are individual, neutral molecules rather than atoms or ions. These molecules are held together by weak intermolecular forces such as London dispersion forces in iodine and wax, or hydrogen bonding in ice, rather than strong chemical bonds. Because these forces are much easier to break than ionic or covalent bonds, molecular solids are typically soft, have low melting points, and do not conduct electricity.

questions.text: Which of the following is covalent crystal?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Iodine forms molecular crystals held together by van der Waals forces, not covalent bonds, so it is not a covalent crystal.
Ice forms hydrogen-bonded molecular crystals, which are not covalent crystals. The primary bonds in ice are hydrogen bonds.
Carborundum, also known as silicon carbide, is a covalent crystal where each silicon atom is covalently bonded to carbon atoms in a strong lattice structure, making it the correct choice.
Magnesium oxide forms ionic crystals due to the ionic bonds between magnesium ions and oxide ions, not covalent bonds.

questions.explanation: Carborundum (silicon carbide) is a covalent crystal because it consists of a network of covalently bonded silicon and carbon atoms. This structure gives it significant strength and high melting point, characteristics of covalent crystals. In contrast, iodine forms molecular crystals with weaker van der Waals forces, ice forms hydrogen-bonded molecular crystals, and magnesium oxide forms ionic crystals with ionic bonds.

questions.text: Which of the following is a polar molecular solid?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: P4 is a non-polar molecular solid because the phosphorus atoms form a symmetrical tetrahedral structure, resulting in an even distribution of charge.
S8 is a non-polar molecular solid as the sulfur atoms form a symmetrical ring structure, leading to a balanced charge distribution.
Sugar, such as sucrose, is a polar molecular solid because it contains polar covalent bonds and an asymmetrical structure, leading to an uneven distribution of charge.
This option is incorrect because sugar is indeed a polar molecular solid.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Sugar. Sugar is a polar molecular solid because it has a structure with polar covalent bonds, such as O-H groups, which result in an uneven distribution of electron density, making it polar. In contrast, P4 and S8 are non-polar molecular solids as they have symmetrical structures, leading to an even distribution of charge. Therefore, they do not exhibit polarity. None of the above is incorrect, as sugar is a valid example of a polar molecular solid.

questions.text: Which of the following crystal conduct electricity in solid form
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Metallic crystals conduct electricity in solid form due to the presence of free electrons.
Molecular crystals do not conduct electricity well in solid form as they generally have no free electrons.
Covalent crystals, like diamond, do not conduct electricity in solid form because their electrons are localized in covalent bonds.
Ionic crystals do not conduct electricity in solid form because their ions are fixed in place in the crystal lattice.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Metallic crystals. Metallic crystals can conduct electricity in solid form due to the presence of delocalized or 'sea of electrons' that are free to move throughout the crystal. This is not the case with molecular, covalent, or ionic crystals. Molecular crystals have neutral molecules with no free charges to carry current. Covalent crystals have electrons that are tightly bound in covalent bonds, preventing them from moving freely. Ionic crystals can conduct electricity only when molten or dissolved in water, as the ions are fixed in place in solid form, preventing them from conducting electricity.

questions.text: Which one is generally soft?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Ionic crystals are composed of positive and negative ions held together by very strong electrostatic forces. These forces create a rigid and tight lattice, making the crystal hard and brittle. While they shatter easily when struck, they are certainly not "soft."

Covalent network crystals (like diamond and quartz) are among the hardest substances known. Every atom is locked in a giant 3D structure by strong, directional covalent bonds. Because these bonds are so difficult to break or bend, these solids are extremely hard.

This is the correct option. Molecular crystals are made of individual molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces (like London dispersion forces or hydrogen bonds). Because these "bridge" forces are much weaker than ionic or covalent bonds, the molecules can easily be moved or displaced, making these solids generally soft and easy to compress.

Metallic crystals have a wide range of hardness. While some (like sodium or potassium) are soft enough to be cut with a knife, most common metals (like iron or chromium) are quite hard. However, as a general category, they are classified as malleable and hard rather than "soft" in the way molecular solids are.

questions.explanation: Hardness depends on the "strength of the glue" holding the particles together. Molecular solids, such as ice, wax, or iodine, are held together by weak van der Waals forces or hydrogen bonds. These are the weakest type of interactions in the solid state, which allows the molecules to slide past each other with very little effort. In contrast, ionic and covalent solids use much stronger internal bonds that lock everything in place, making them much harder to scratch or dent. This is why you can easily scratch a candle (wax) with your fingernail, but you cannot do the same to a salt crystal or a diamond.

questions.text: Which of the following is ẞ-sulphur?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Rhombic sulphur is the most stable allotrope of sulphur at room temperature, but it is not referred to as ẞ-sulphur.
Monoclinic sulphur, also known as beta-sulphur (ẞ-sulphur), is stable at higher temperatures and converts to rhombic sulphur upon cooling.
Plastic sulphur is an amorphous form of sulphur obtained by rapidly cooling molten sulphur. It does not have a crystalline structure like ẞ-sulphur.
Amorphous sulphur lacks a definite crystalline structure and is not categorized as ẞ-sulphur.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Monoclinic sulphur. Monoclinic sulphur, or beta-sulphur (ẞ-sulphur), is an allotrope of sulphur that is stable at temperatures above 96°C and converts to rhombic sulphur upon cooling. Rhombic sulphur is stable at room temperature but is not called ẞ-sulphur. Plastic sulphur and amorphous sulphur do not have the crystalline structure characteristic of ẞ-sulphur.

questions.text: The number of crystallographic elements of any unit cell is
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: A unit cell cannot be defined by just two elements. In geometry, two parameters could only define a line or a simple 2D shape like a rectangle (length and width), but a crystal exists in three-dimensional space and requires more information to describe its volume.

Four elements are insufficient to describe the full 3D geometry of a crystal. While some specific crystal systems, like the cubic system, can be identified by fewer unique values (since a=b=c and all angles are 90°), the standard set of parameters used to define any general unit cell is larger than four.

This is the correct option. A unit cell is characterized by six crystallographic elements (also known as lattice parameters). These include the lengths of the three edges (a, b, c) and the three interfacial angles between them (⍺, β, ˠ). Together, these six values define the size and shape of the unit cell in 3D space.

Eight elements are not the standard for defining a unit cell. While a cube has eight corners, the dimensions and geometry are fully fixed once the three edge lengths and three angles are known. Adding more parameters would be redundant for describing the basic shape.

questions.explanation: a unit cell is described as the smallest repeating block of a crystal. To perfectly define this "box" in three dimensions, we need exactly six parameters. Three of these are the lengths of the edges (a, b, and c) along the three axes, and the other three are the angles (α, β, and ˠ) between those edges. By changing these six values, we can create all seven crystal systems, such as cubic, tetragonal, or triclinic. Without all six of these elements, we wouldn't know if the box is stretched, tilted, or perfectly square.

questions.text: One centre of symmetry is concerned with
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: A simple cubic crystal has a center of symmetry because it is based on a cubic geometry. If you start at any point on the crystal's surface and draw a line through the exact center of the cube, you will find an identical point at an equal distance on the opposite side. This "point of inversion" exists regardless of whether atoms are only at the corners.

In a body-centered cubic structure, there is an actual atom located at the center of the unit cell. This central point serves as the center of symmetry. Any line passing through this central atom will hit identical corner atoms at equal distances in opposite directions, maintaining the symmetry.

Face-centered cubic crystals also possess a center of symmetry. Although the center of the cube does not contain an atom in this arrangement, the mathematical "center point" of the cubic volume still acts as a center of symmetry for the corner and face-centered atoms surrounding it.

This is the correct option. A center of symmetry (also called an inversion center) is a property of the cubic crystal system itself. Since simple cubic, body-centered cubic, and face-centered cubic are all members of the cubic system, they all possess exactly one center of symmetry located at the geometric center of the unit cell.

questions.explanation: A center of symmetry is an imaginary point inside a crystal such that any line passing through it will connect two identical points on opposite sides of the crystal at the same distance. In the cubic system, this point is always located at the very center of the cube. Because simple cubic, body-centered, and face-centered structures are all just different ways of filling a cube-shaped "box," they all share this same central symmetry point. Even if the center of the box is empty (like in simple cubic or face-centered cubic), the geometric symmetry remains the same.

questions.text: Total number of atoms (particles) per unit cell of a face centered cubic system is
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is incorrect. A face-centered cubic unit cell contains more than one atom per unit cell because atoms are positioned at each corner and the center of each face.
This is correct. A face-centered cubic unit cell contains 8 corner atoms, each contributing 1/8th, and 6 face atoms, each contributing 1/2, resulting in a total of 4 atoms per unit cell.
This is incorrect. 8 atoms refers to the number of corner atoms only, but they do not solely define the total number of atoms in a face-centered cubic unit cell.
This is incorrect. 14 atoms are present in a body-centered cubic unit cell, not in a face-centered cubic unit cell.

questions.explanation: In a face-centered cubic (FCC) unit cell, atoms are located at each of the 8 corners of the cube and at the center of each of the 6 faces. Each corner atom is shared among 8 adjacent unit cells, contributing 1/8th of an atom per unit cell, resulting in a total contribution of 1 atom from the corners (8 * 1/8 = 1). Each face atom is shared between 2 adjacent unit cells, contributing 1/2 of an atom per unit cell, resulting in a total contribution of 3 atoms from the face atoms (6 * 1/2 = 3). Therefore, the total number of atoms per unit cell in an FCC structure is 4 (1 from corners + 3 from faces).Option A is incorrect because it underestimates the contributions from the face atoms. Option C is incorrect because 8 refers only to the number of corner atoms, without considering the division among unit cells. Option D is incorrect because 14 refers to the total number of atoms in a body-centered cubic structure, not an FCC structure.

questions.text: Number of particles per unit cell of simple cubic system
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: In a simple cubic system, each corner atom is shared by eight unit cells, resulting in a total of 1 atom per unit cell.
This option mistakenly assumes a contribution from atoms other than the corner atoms, which is not the case for a simple cubic system.
This represents the number of atoms in a face-centered cubic unit cell, not a simple cubic system.
This is the number associated with a body-centered cubic unit cell, which is different from a simple cubic system.

questions.explanation: In a simple cubic system, each corner of the cube contains one-eighth of an atom, as each atom is shared by eight adjacent unit cells. Therefore, the total number of atoms per unit cell is 8 corners × 1/8 atom per corner = 1 atom. Options B, C, and D are incorrect because they describe different crystal structures: Option B does not apply to any standard cubic system; Option C corresponds to a face-centered cubic system, and Option D is for a body-centered cubic system.

questions.text: The geometry of diamond crystal is
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is the correct geometrical structure of a diamond. In a face-centered cubic lattice, atoms are located at each corner and the centers of all the faces of the cube.
This structure has atoms at each corner and a single atom at the center of the cube. This is not the arrangement found in a diamond crystal.
In a simple cubic structure, atoms are located only at the corners of the cube, which differs from the complex arrangement in a diamond.
The triclinic system is the least symmetrical of the seven crystal systems. It does not describe the highly symmetrical cubic structure of a diamond.

questions.explanation: Diamond crystals have a face-centered cubic (FCC) structure. This means that in addition to the atoms at the corners of the cube, there are additional atoms at the center of each face of the cube. This arrangement results in a very strong and rigid lattice, contributing to diamond's renowned hardness. The other options describe different types of crystal lattices that do not match the atomic arrangement of diamond. The body-centered cubic structure has an atom in the center of the cube, which is not present in diamonds. The simple cubic structure lacks the additional atoms on the faces of the cube. The triclinic system is entirely different and does not apply to the symmetrical cubic nature of diamond crystals.

questions.text: Crystal in which carbon atoms occupy lattice points are
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Covalent crystals, like diamond, have lattice points occupied by carbon atoms bonded covalently.
Ionic crystals are composed of ions, not atoms like carbon, and are bonded by electrostatic forces.
Molecular crystals consist of molecules held together by weak forces, not individual atoms like carbon.
Metallic crystals have lattice points occupied by metal atoms bonded by a 'sea' of delocalized electrons.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is covalent crystals. In covalent crystals, such as diamond, the lattice points are occupied by carbon atoms bonded covalently to each other, creating a strong and rigid structure. In contrast, ionic crystals are composed of positive and negative ions held together by electrostatic forces, molecular crystals are formed by molecules held together by weaker van der Waals forces or hydrogen bonds, and metallic crystals consist of metal atoms with delocalized electrons facilitating metallic bonding.

questions.text: Which of the following parameters stands for diamond unit cell?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: a = b = c, α = β ≠ γ = 90°
options.explanation: This configuration does not align with any known crystal system.

options.text: a ≠ b ≠ c, α = β = γ = 90°
options.explanation: This describes an orthorhombic crystal system, which is not suitable for diamond.

options.text: a = b = c, α = β = γ = 90°
options.explanation: This is the description of a cubic crystal system, which matches the structure of a diamond unit cell.

options.text: a = b = c, α ≠ β ≠ γ ≠ 90°
options.explanation: This describes a system with no right angles, which does not fit the cubic structure of diamond.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C:a = b = c, α = β = γ = 90°. This configuration describes a cubic crystal system, characteristic of the diamond unit cell. In a cubic system, all sides are equal, and all angles are right angles (90°). The other options do not match the cubic structure: Option A has unequal angles, Option B describes an orthorhombic system, and Option D suggests a non-cubic system.

questions.text: a ≠ b ≠ c, α≠ β≠ γ≠ 90° represents
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: In a cubic system, all sides are equal (a = b = c) and all angles are 90°. This does not match the given parameters.
In a monoclinic system, two angles are 90° and one is not (α = γ = 90°, β ≠ 90°), which does not match the given parameters.
In a triclinic system, all sides are unequal (a ≠ b ≠ c) and all angles are unequal and not 90° (α≠ β≠ γ≠ 90°), matching the given parameters.
In a hexagonal system, two sides are equal (a = b ≠ c) and angles are α = β = 90°, γ = 120°, which does not match the given parameters.

questions.explanation: The triclinic system is characterized by having all sides of different lengths (a ≠ b ≠ c) and all angles being different and not 90° (α≠ β≠ γ≠ 90°), which corresponds exactly to the conditions described in the question. The other systems, such as cubic, monoclinic, and hexagonal, have specific equalities in sides or angles that do not fit these conditions.

questions.text: Which one is trigonal crystal?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: BaSO4·4H2O does not typically crystallize in the trigonal system.
FeSO4 usually forms monoclinic crystals, not trigonal.
NaCl crystallizes in a cubic system, not trigonal.
NaNO3 crystallizes in the trigonal system, making it the correct answer.

questions.explanation: NaNO₃ is the correct answer as it crystallizes in the trigonal system. The trigonal system is characterized by a single threefold axis of rotation. BaSO₄·4H₂O, FeSO₄, and NaCl crystallize in different systems (orthorhombic, monoclinic, and cubic respectively) and do not match the properties of the trigonal crystal system.

questions.text: The unit cell of NaCl is.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: NaCl does not form a simple cubic structure. In a simple cubic structure, each corner atom is shared by eight unit cells, but NaCl has a more complex arrangement.
This is the correct answer. In NaCl, each sodium ion is surrounded by six chloride ions and vice versa, forming a face-centered cubic lattice.
NaCl does not have a body-centered cubic structure. This type of structure includes an additional atom at the center of the cube, which is not present in NaCl's lattice.
NaCl does not crystallize in a hexagonal structure. Hexagonal structures are typical of other types of compounds, such as graphite or metals like magnesium.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Face-centered cubic type. NaCl crystallizes in a face-centered cubic (FCC) lattice structure, where each sodium ion is surrounded by six chloride ions and each chloride ion is surrounded by six sodium ions. This arrangement maximizes the electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions, making it a highly stable structure.Option A, Simple cubic type, is incorrect because NaCl does not form a simple cubic structure as it requires a more complex arrangement to stabilize its ionic character.Option C, Body centered cubic type, is incorrect because a body-centered cubic structure involves an additional atom in the center of the cube, which is not how NaCl is structured.Option D, Hexagonal type, is incorrect because NaCl does not crystallize in a hexagonal form; this structure is characteristic of other materials, not ionic compounds like NaCl.

questions.text: The packing pattern in NaCl is
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is the correct option. In NaCl, each Na+ ion is surrounded by six Cl- ions in an octahedral geometry, forming a face-centered cubic (FCC) structure, which is a type of cubic close packing.
This is incorrect. Body centered cubic (BCC) packing involves a different arrangement where each atom is surrounded by eight others, which is not the case in NaCl.
This is incorrect. Hexagonal close packing is a different structure where the layers of atoms are arranged in a hexagonal pattern, not typical for NaCl.
This is incorrect. Square close packing is not a recognized term in the context of three-dimensional packing. It's more related to two-dimensional structures.

questions.explanation: In the crystal structure of NaCl, the ions are arranged in a face-centered cubic (FCC) lattice. Each sodium ion is coordinated by six chloride ions and vice versa, forming an octahedral coordination environment. This type of arrangement is known as cubic close packing, which is common for many ionic compounds. The other options describe different packing structures: body centered cubic (BCC) and hexagonal close packing (HCP) do not apply to NaCl, and square close packing is not relevant in three-dimensional solids.

questions.text: In NaCl crystal, each ion is surrounded by six oppositely charged ions so that the arrangement is
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: In a tetrahedral arrangement, an ion is surrounded by four oppositely charged ions, forming a shape with four triangular faces.
A trigonal bipyramidal arrangement involves an ion surrounded by five oppositely charged ions, forming a structure with two triangular pyramids sharing a base.
A square planar arrangement involves an ion surrounded by four oppositely charged ions at the corners of a square.
In an octahedral arrangement, each ion is surrounded by six oppositely charged ions, forming a shape with eight faces, like in the NaCl crystal.

questions.explanation: In the NaCl crystal, each sodium ion (Na+) is surrounded by six chloride ions (Cl-), and each chloride ion is surrounded by six sodium ions. This arrangement forms an octahedral structure, which is characteristic of the NaCl crystal lattice. The octahedral arrangement allows for maximum electrostatic attraction between the oppositely charged ions, stabilizing the crystal. The other options describe structures with fewer than six surrounding ions, making them incorrect for the NaCl crystal.

questions.text: In which of the following there is a point which is part of only one unit cell?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Atoms are present only at the corners. Each corner atom is shared by 8 unit cells, so no point belongs to only one unit cell.
One atom is present at the body center, and this atom is completely inside one unit cell only (not shared).
Face-center atoms are shared by 2 unit cells, so they are not part of only one unit cell.
Atoms are shared between multiple unit cells; none belong exclusively to one unit cell.

questions.explanation: In crystallography, we determine how many cells "share" a specific point based on its position. Points on corners are the most shared (8 cells), while points on faces are shared by two. However, a point located in the absolute center of the unit cell—like the one found in an I-type (Body-centered) lattice—is not on a boundary. Because it sits deep inside the "box," it is not shared with any neighbors, making it the only point that belongs 100% to that single unit cell.

questions.text: The point at corner of the cubic unit cell is shared with
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Incorrect. In a cubic structure, each corner atom is shared by 8 adjacent unit cells.
Incorrect. While cubic unit cells do have 6 faces, corner atoms are shared by more than 6 cells.
Correct. Each corner of a cubic unit cell is shared by 8 neighboring unit cells.
Incorrect. 2 unit cells share an edge, not a corner in a cubic structure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C: 8 unit cells. In a cubic lattice, each corner of the unit cell is shared by 8 adjacent unit cells. This is because each corner atom is situated at the intersection of 8 unit cells that surround the corner in a 3D space. This sharing arrangement is fundamental to the structure of cubic lattices, ensuring that each corner contributes equally to the neighboring cells. The other options are incorrect because they do not accurately represent how many unit cells share a corner in a cubic lattice.

questions.text: Which of the following unit cell may have a point which is equidistance from all the faces.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids
