options.explanation: A triclinic unit cell has no axes of symmetry, making it impossible for a point to be equidistant from all faces.
In a monoclinic unit cell, only one axis is perpendicular to the base, which prevents a point from being equidistant from all faces.
A tetragonal unit cell has two axes of equal length, but it does not provide the symmetry required for equidistance from all faces.
The cubic unit cell is highly symmetrical, with all axes and faces being equal, allowing for a point to be equidistant from all faces.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: Cubic. In a cubic unit cell, the symmetry and equal length of all axes ensure that there is a central point that is equidistant from all the faces of the cube. This is not possible in triclinic, monoclinic, or tetragonal cells due to their lack of symmetrical equivalence across axes or faces.

questions.text: Three dimensional arrangement of particles in the solids is called
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: A unit cell is only the smallest repeating portion of a crystal. While it is three-dimensional, it is not the name for the entire arrangement itself. Think of the unit cell as a single "brick," whereas the whole arrangement is the "brick wall."

This is the correct option. A lattice (specifically a crystal lattice or space lattice) is the complete, regular three-dimensional arrangement of particles (atoms, ions, or molecules) in a solid. It represents the infinite pattern that defines how the substance is built in space.

A lattice site refers to the specific position or "seat" in the crystal lattice where a particle is actually located. It is a part of the lattice, but it does not refer to the overall 3D arrangement of all the particles together.

A lattice point is a mathematical representation of a particle's position in the lattice. Each point in a lattice is called a lattice point. While the lattice is made of these points, the term "lattice" is the one used to describe the entire 3D structure.

questions.explanation: We define a crystal lattice as a regular array of points in three-dimensional space. If you imagine every atom or ion in a solid as a tiny dot and connect those dots with lines, the resulting 3D "skeleton" is the lattice. It shows the symmetry and the repeating nature of the solid. While a unit cell is the smallest sample of this pattern, the lattice is the total map of how thousands of these cells stack together to form a visible crystal.

questions.text: In which of the following, there is a point which is part of only two adjacent unit cells?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: A cubic P-type (Primitive) lattice has lattice points only at the corners of the cube. As discussed previously, each corner point is shared among eight adjacent unit cells. Therefore, it does not contain any points that belong to only two adjacent cells.

A Cubic I-type (Body-centered) lattice has corner points (shared by 8 cells) and one center point. As you know, the center point belongs to only one unit cell. It does not have any point that is shared between exactly two cells.

This is the correct option. A Cubic F-type (Face-centered) lattice has points at the corners and at the center of each of its six faces. A point located at the center of a face sits exactly on the boundary between two adjacent unit cells. Therefore, each face-centered point is shared by only two cells.
This option is incorrect because the F-type lattice specifically contains points (the face-centered points) that meet the requirement of being shared by exactly two adjacent unit cells.

questions.explanation: In crystal chemistry, the location of a particle determines how many "neighbors" share it. Corner points are shared by 8 cells, and body-center points are not shared at all. However, a point in an F-type (Face-centered) lattice is located on the flat face of the cube. Since a face is like a wall between two rooms, any atom placed there is split exactly in half, with one half belonging to one unit cell and the other half belonging to the neighbor right next to it. This is why we say face-centered atoms are shared by only two adjacent unit cells.

questions.text: The unit cell of chilli salt peter(NaNO3)is
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: It requires all axes to be equal and all angles to be 90°, which NaNO₃ does not have.

Sodium nitrate, NaNO3, crystallizes in the hexagonal (trigonal) crystal system. Its structure shows three equal axes at 120° in one plane and a fourth axis perpendicular to that plane, which is characteristic of the hexagonal system.
Orthorhombic structures have a different symmetry compared to the cubic system of NaNO₃.
Axes are unequal with all angles 90°, not applicable to NaNO3.

questions.explanation: Chile saltpeter (NaNO3) crystallizes in the trigonal system, which is very similar to the structure of calcite (CaCO3). In many textbooks, the trigonal system is treated as a subtype of the hexagonal system because they both involve similar geometry. The unit cell looks like a cube that has been stretched along one of its diagonals, resulting in equal side lengths but angles that are not $90$ degrees.

questions.text: Water, being a unique substance, can exist in all three states in nature. The smallest volume exhibited by a given mass of water is observed at
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: At 0 K, water would theoretically be a solid in a hexagonal crystal structure, which is less dense than liquid water at 4°C. This is because the rigid hexagonal structure of ice contains more empty space.
At 273 K (0°C), water begins to solidify, forming ice, which is less dense than liquid water at 4°C. This transition involves the formation of a rigid structure with some empty space.
At 277 K (4°C), water reaches its maximum density, meaning it occupies the smallest volume for a given mass. This is due to the optimal arrangement of water molecules at this temperature.
At 298 K (25°C), water is less dense than at 4°C because the increased kinetic energy causes water molecules to occupy a larger volume.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 277 K (4°C) because this is the temperature at which water reaches its maximum density, resulting in the smallest volume for a given mass. Water molecules are optimally packed at this temperature, minimizing the volume. At 0 K, water would form a solid structure with more empty space, making it less dense. At 273 K, ice begins to form, which is also less dense than liquid water at 4°C due to its rigid structure. At 298 K, water molecules have more kinetic energy, causing them to spread out and occupy a larger volume.

questions.text: For orthorhombic system axial ratios are a ≠ b ≠ c and the axial angles are:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option does not represent the axial angles for an orthorhombic crystal system, where all angles should be equal to 90˚.
Correct. In the orthorhombic system, the axial angles are α = β = γ = 90˚, even though the axial ratios are a ≠ b ≠ c.
This configuration does not apply to the orthorhombic crystal system, as all axial angles should be 90˚.
This setup contradicts the defining characteristic of the orthorhombic system, which has all angles at 90˚.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: α = β = γ = 90˚. In the orthorhombic crystal system, while the axial lengths a, b, and c are different from each other (a ≠ b ≠ c), the axial angles are all right angles, meaning α = β = γ = 90˚. This is a key characteristic that distinguishes the orthorhombic system from other crystal systems.Option A is incorrect because it suggests unequal angles, which is not the case for orthorhombic crystals. Option C is incorrect as it proposes that one angle is not 90˚, which contradicts the orthorhombic definition. Option D is incorrect because it suggests all angles are not equal to 90˚, which is entirely inaccurate for this system.

questions.text: Solid carbon dioxide (dry ice) is used as a refrigerating agent because it readily changes directly from the solid into the vapor state at low temperature. What does this indicate the main intermolecular bonding / forces in CO2(s) to be?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Covalent bonding involves the sharing of electrons between atoms, forming strong bonds within a molecule. These are intramolecular forces and do not explain the weak forces between CO2 molecules in the solid state.
Ionic bonding occurs between positively and negatively charged ions. CO2 is a molecular compound with no ionic character, so ionic bonding is not relevant here.

Hydrogen bonding is a strong type of dipole-dipole interaction that occurs between molecules where hydrogen is bonded to highly electronegative elements like N, O, or F. CO2 does not contain hydrogen atoms, so hydrogen bonding is not applicable.

London dispersion forces are temporary, weak intermolecular forces present in all molecules, including nonpolar ones like CO2. These forces are responsible for the sublimation of dry ice as they are the only forces present between CO2 molecules.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct.The main intermolecular bonding forces in CO2(s) are London dispersion forces. London dispersion forces are the weakest type of intermolecular force. They occur between all molecules, but they are only significant when the molecules are large or have highly electronegative atoms. Carbon dioxide is a nonpolar molecule, so it does not have dipole-dipole forces or hydrogen bonding. However, the carbon dioxide molecules are polar because of the difference in electronegativity between carbon and oxygen. This difference in electronegativity creates a temporary dipole in each molecule, which can induce a dipole in neighboring molecules. The interaction between these induced dipoles is what creates London dispersion forces. The strength of London dispersion forces increases with the number of electrons in a molecule. Carbon dioxide has 12 valence electrons, which is relatively high for a nonpolar molecule. This means that the London dispersion forces in CO2(s) are relatively strong. The strong London dispersion forces in CO2(s) make it difficult for the molecules to move past each other. This is why solid carbon dioxide is a solid at room temperature.

questions.text: A student mislabels three jars containing three different molecules. The student frantically tries to find the identity of the molecules in each jar. He knows that the three possible molecules are methanol, dichloromethane, and propane. At room temperature, he observes that one of the jars contains a gas, whereas the other two jars contain liquids. He then finds the boiling point of each jar. The molecule from jar A has a boiling point of 39.6°C, jar B has a boiling point of -42°C, and jar C has a boiling point of 64.7°C. Based on his findings, he can determine the identity of the molecules in each jar.What is the identity of Jar B?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: Dichloromethane, because it only contains dipole-dipole interactions, has the strongest intermolecular force
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because Dichloromethane (CH2Cl2) has a boiling point of 39.6 °C, which matches Jar A. While it does have dipole-dipole interactions, these are not the strongest intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonding is stronger), and more importantly, its boiling point is much higher than -42 °C.

options.text: Propane, because it only contains London dispersion forces, the weakest intermolecular force
options.explanation: This is the correct option. Propane (C3H8) is a nonpolar hydrocarbon. Because it lacks polar bonds or hydrogen atoms attached to N, O, or F, the only forces holding its molecules together are London dispersion forces. These are the weakest type of intermolecular force, which explains why propane has such a low boiling point (-42 °C) and exists as a gas at room temperature.

options.text: Propane, because it only contains London dispersion forces, the strongest intermolecular force
options.explanation: While it correctly identifies the molecule as propane, the reasoning is wrong. London dispersion forces are the weakest intermolecular forces, not the strongest. If they were the strongest, propane would have a very high boiling point and be a solid or liquid, rather than a gas.

options.text: Dichloromethane, because it only contains dipole-dipole interactions, the weakest intermolecular force
options.explanation: This is incorrect because it misidentifies the molecule in Jar B. Dichloromethane is a liquid at room temperature with a much higher boiling point than propane. Additionally, while dipole-dipole forces are relatively weak compared to hydrogen bonds, they are generally stronger than London dispersion forces for molecules of similar size.

questions.explanation: To solve this, we look at the strength of the "glue" (intermolecular forces) holding the molecules together.Methanol (Jar C, 64.7 °C): Has hydrogen bonding, the strongest force here, giving it the highest boiling point.Dichloromethane (Jar A, 39.6 °C): Is polar and has dipole-dipole forces, which are medium strength.Propane (Jar B, -42 °C): Is non-polar and only has London dispersion forces. Because these forces are so weak, the molecules barely stick together and fly apart into a gas at very low temperatures. This is why Jar B, with the lowest boiling point, must be propane.

questions.text: A tin-can is sealed at a pressure of 200 mmHg. If the can is taken to standard pressure, the can may
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: An explosion occurs when the internal pressure of a container is much higher than the external (atmospheric) pressure, causing the walls to burst outward. In this case, the internal pressure (200 mmHg) is much lower than the standard external pressure (760mmHg), so the force is pushing inward, not outward.

Bursting is similar to exploding; it happens when a container can no longer hold its contents due to high internal pressure. Since the pressure outside the can is higher than the pressure inside, the can will not burst outward.

Enlarging would require the gas inside to expand and push the walls of the tin can outward. This happens if you move a sealed container from a high-pressure area to a low-pressure area (like taking a bag of chips up a mountain). Here, the opposite is happening.

This is the correct option. Standard atmospheric pressure is 760mmHg. Since the can was sealed at only 200mmHg, the air outside is pressing against the can with much more force than the air inside is pushing back. This huge pressure difference (560mmHg of crushing force) will cause the walls of the tin can to cave in or shrink.

questions.explanation: Gases exert pressure in all directions. For a container to keep its shape, the pressure pushing out from the inside must be roughly equal to the atmospheric pressure pushing in from the outside. Standard pressure at sea level is 760mmHg. If you seal a can at a very low pressure (200mmHg) and then expose it to the normal atmosphere, the outside air acts like a giant invisible hand squeezing the can. Because the inside "push" is too weak to fight back, the can will buckle and shrink under the weight of the atmosphere.

questions.text: If a liquid is continuously heated at its boiling point then its boiling point will
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Increasing the heat supply will make the liquid boil faster (more bubbles and steam), but it will not increase the temperature of the boiling liquid. As long as liquid is present and the atmospheric pressure is constant, the temperature is capped at the boiling point.

The boiling point is a physical property determined by the atmospheric pressure and the nature of the liquid. Heating the liquid does not reduce this value; it only provides the energy required to reach it and then maintain the phase change.

This is the correct option. Once a liquid reaches its boiling point, its temperature remains constant until all the liquid has converted into vapor. All the extra heat energy being added is used as "latent heat" to break the intermolecular forces between molecules, rather than increasing their kinetic energy (temperature).

While 100°C is the boiling point of pure water at sea level, this is not true for all liquids (like alcohol or oil) or for water at different altitudes. The question asks what happens to a liquid's boiling point, which remains constant at its own specific value, not necessarily 100°C.

questions.explanation: It is crucial to understand that once a liquid reaches its boiling point, its temperature stays fixed, even if you keep heating it. This happens because the energy you are adding is no longer being used to increase the speed of the molecules (which would raise the temperature). Instead, that energy is used to overcome the attractive forces between the molecules so they can break free and become a gas. This "hidden" heat is called the latent heat of vaporization. The thermometer will only show a rise in temperature again after the very last drop of liquid has completely evaporated into steam.

questions.text: If the molecular mass of an ideal gas is twice the molecular mass of carbon dioxide, the volume occupied by the ideal gas at STP is
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This volume (11.2dm3) represents half of a mole of gas at STP. It would only be the correct answer if the question stated we had 0.5moles of the gas, which is not mentioned here.

This is the correct option. According to Avogadro's Law, one mole of any ideal gas at Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP) occupies exactly 22.414dm3. Since the question refers to "the ideal gas" (implying 1 mole unless stated otherwise), the molar volume remains constant regardless of the gas's molecular mass

This volume (44.8dm3) represents two moles of an ideal gas at STP. While the molecular mass of this gas is twice that of CO2, the volume of a gas depends on the number of particles (moles), not how heavy the individual particles are.
This number (44) is the numerical value of the molecular mass of carbon dioxide (CO2 = 12 + 16 x 2 = 44g/mol). It is a mass value and does not represent a volume in dm3 for an ideal gas at STP.

questions.explanation: At STP (0°C and 1 atm), 1 mole of any ideal gas occupies a volume of 22.4 dm³, regardless of its molecular mass.Given:Molecular mass of the gas = 2 × molecular mass of CO₂Molecular mass of CO₂ = 44 g mol⁻¹So, gas molecular mass = 88 g mol⁻¹This information does not affect the volume at STP, because volume depends only on the number of moles, not on molecular mass.

questions.text: Which of the following is the strongest intermolecular force found among liquids?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: London forces (or dispersion forces) are the weakest type of intermolecular force. They are temporary attractions that occur in all molecules, especially non-polar ones, due to the constant movement of electrons. Because they are short-lived and weak, they do not provide strong sticking power between molecules.

Cohesive forces is a general term that refers to all attractive forces between like molecules in a liquid. While it describes the tendency of molecules to stick together, it is not a specific type of chemical force like the others listed; rather, it is a category that includes London forces, dipole-dipole, and hydrogen bonding.

This is the correct choice. Hydrogen bonding is a particularly strong type of dipole-dipole interaction that occurs when hydrogen is bonded to highly electronegative atoms like Nitrogen, Oxygen, or Fluorine. It is much stronger than regular dipole-dipole forces or London forces because of the large difference in electronegativity and the small size of the hydrogen atom.

Dipole-dipole interactions occur between polar molecules that have permanent positive and negative ends. While these are stronger than London forces, they are generally much weaker than hydrogen bonds. They only involve moderate partial charges rather than the intense attraction seen in hydrogen bonding.

questions.explanation: Intermolecular forces are ranked by their strength. Hydrogen bonding stands at the top of this list for simple molecular liquids. It acts like a very strong "molecular glue" because the hydrogen atom gets a strong positive charge when attached to atoms like oxygen. This positive hydrogen is then powerfully attracted to the lone pairs of electrons on neighboring molecules. This is why water, which has strong hydrogen bonds, has such a high boiling point compared to other liquids of similar size.

questions.text: What is the volume of one mole of helium gas at a pressure of 55 N/m2 and temperature 120°C? [Note: R = 8.314 JK−1 mol−1 ]
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This value is much too small for the given conditions. A volume of 0.02m3 is approximately the space occupied by one mole of gas at high pressure or very low temperature, but here the pressure is extremely low (55N/m2), which allows the gas to expand significantly.

This value does not match any simple miscalculation involving the standard units. It is likely a distractor for students who might confuse the units of m3 with dm3 or use an incorrect value for the gas constant R.
As per calculation (refer to the calculation below) the answer is 55.41 m3. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Use the formula PV = nRTConvert 120°C into kelvin; 120 + 273 = 393KThe value for P is 55; the value for n is 1; the value for R is 8.314V = nrt/p = (1 x 8.341 x 393)/ 55 = 59.41

questions.explanation: Use the formula PV = nRTConvert 120°C into kelvin; 120 + 273 = 393KThe value for P is 55; the value for n is 1; the value for R is 8.314V = nrt/p = (1 x 8.341 x 393)/ 55 = 59.41 m3

questions.text: When water is poured into a glass test tube, the surface of the water is concave. This meniscus forms because of:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: If the adhesive forces (the attraction between the liquid and the glass) were weak, the water would not want to touch the walls of the tube. Instead, it would pull away and try to form a ball in the center, which would not result in a concave shape.

This is the correct option. A concave meniscus forms because the adhesive forces between the water molecules and the glass walls are stronger than the cohesive forces between the water molecules themselves. Because water "prefers" to stick to the glass more than to itself, it actually climbs up the sides of the tube, creating the curved downward dip in the middle.

Strong cohesive forces (attraction between like molecules) actually cause a convex meniscus. This is seen in mercury, where the atoms are so strongly attracted to each other that they pull away from the glass, creating a dome-like or upward-curving shape.

If the adhesive forces and cohesive forces were exactly equal, the surface of the liquid would remain perfectly flat or horizontal. There would be no tendency for the liquid to either climb the walls or pull away from them, resulting in no curvature at all.

questions.explanation: The shape of the liquid surface (meniscus) is a battle between two forces: adhesion and cohesion. For water in glass, the glass is very polar, so water molecules "stick" to it very strongly through adhesion. Because this attraction to the glass is stronger than the internal "pull" of water molecules on each other (cohesion), the water edges crawl upward. This result is the bowl-shaped or concave surface that we always measure from the bottom.

questions.text: At 4°C, water has a tendency to exhibit maximum:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Weight is the force exerted by gravity on a mass (W = mg). While the mass of water remains constant regardless of temperature, gravity does not change based on whether water is at 4°C or 10°C, so the weight does not reach a "maximum" in this context.

This is the correct option. At 4°C (277K), water molecules are packed more closely together than at any other temperature. This state of being "most tightly packed" means it has its maximum density (exactly 1.000g/cm3). Above or below this temperature, water expands and becomes less dense.
The density of water is maximum at 4 degrees Celsius hence its volume is minimum due to the formula density = mass/ volume. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Pressure in a liquid is generally determined by depth or external force, not by the specific internal temperature of 4°C. While temperature can affect the pressure of a gas in a closed container, it is not a defining characteristic of water reaching a "maximum" at this specific temperature.

questions.explanation: Water behaves in a very unusual way called "anomalous expansion." Most liquids keep shrinking as they get colder, but water stops shrinking at 4°C. At this point, the molecules are at their closest possible distance. If you cool it further toward 0°C, the molecules start to form a loose, cage-like structure that actually pushes them apart, making the water expand. Because 4°C is the point where the molecules are most crowded, it is the temperature where water has its highest density.

So, the answer is B.

So, the answer is B.

questions.text: Substance Z is at 0.5 atm and 200 K. If the pressure on substance Z is steadily increased and its temperature is kept constant, what phase change will eventually occur?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is the correct choice. When you increase the pressure on a gas while keeping the temperature constant, the molecules are forced closer together. Eventually, they get close enough for intermolecular forces to take over, causing the gas to turn into a liquid. This process is called condensation.

Freezing is the change from a liquid to a solid. While increasing pressure can sometimes cause freezing (if the solid is denser than the liquid), this usually happens at much higher pressures or different starting points. Starting from 0.5atm (a low pressure), the first transition for a gas is almost always to the liquid state.

Melting is the transition from a solid to a liquid, which usually requires an increase in temperature. Increasing the pressure on a substance at a constant, low temperature is the opposite of what typically causes melting; in fact, pressure usually favors the formation of the more compact phase (solid).

Sublimation is the direct change from a solid to a gas. Since the question describes a process starting at a low pressure and increasing it, you are moving toward a more condensed state (liquid or solid), not toward a gas. Sublimation would occur if you were decreasing the pressure.
Vaporization refers to the phase change from liquid to gas, and requires a decrease in pressure. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: At standard temperature when the pressure increases, substances undergo phase changes from gas to liquid to solid. Condensation is the phase change from gas to liquid. As in the question, there is an increase in the pressure while the temperature is constant, and the process of condensation is occurring. So option A is the correct answer.

questions.text: A substance possessing a characteristically low vapor pressure can be expected to have:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: If a substance had extremely weak intermolecular forces, the molecules would easily escape from the surface into the vapor phase. This would result in a very high vapor pressure, not a low one. Substances like petrol or ether have weak forces and thus very high vapor pressures.

options.text: A relatively small heat of vaporization
options.explanation: Heat of vaporization is the energy required to transform a liquid into a gas. A small heat of vaporization means it takes very little energy to break the molecules apart. Such substances evaporate very easily and would therefore have a high vapor pressure, which is the opposite of what the question describes.

This is the correct option. A low vapor pressure indicates that the molecules are held together by very strong attractive forces. Because these forces are so strong, you must heat the liquid to a much higher temperature before its vapor pressure equals the atmospheric pressure. Therefore, substances with low vapor pressure always have relatively high boiling points.

options.text: A relatively high rate of evaporation
options.explanation: The rate of evaporation is directly related to vapor pressure. If a substance has a low vapor pressure, it means very few molecules are escaping into the air at a given temperature. Consequently, such a substance will have a very slow rate of evaporation, like honey or heavy oil.

options.text: A significantly high percentage of molecules that have high kinetic energy
options.explanation: The kinetic energy of molecules in a substance is related to its temperature, not its vapor pressure. While high kinetic energy may contribute to a higher vapor pressure, it does not necessarily indicate a low vapor pressure. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Vapor pressure is defined as the pressure exerted by the vapors of a liquid in equilibrium with its liquid state. If the "internal glue" (intermolecular forces) holding the liquid together is very strong, the molecules find it very difficult to escape into the air. This results in very few vapor molecules and a low vapor pressure. Because these molecules are so tightly bound, you need to provide a lot of heat to get them to boil, which is why a low vapor pressure always points to a high boiling point.

questions.text: An ideal gas in a closed inflexible container has a pressure of 6 atmospheres and a temperature of 27˚C. What will be the new pressure of the gas if the temperature is decreased to –73˚C?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: As per calculations the answer is 4 atm (refer to the calculation below). Hence, this option is incorrect.
As per calculations the answer is 4 atm (refer to the calculation below). Hence, this option is incorrect.

The pressure and temperature of an ideal gas are related by the gay-lussac’s law, which states that pressure exerted on a gas varies directly with the absolute temperature of a gas. It is given by the equation: P1 / T1 = P2 / T2Where, P1 = 6 atmospheres. T1 = 27˚C in kelvin: 27+273 = 300K T2 = –73˚C in kelvin: -73+273 = 200K P2 = unknown (to be found)So, we can use this to find the new pressure, i.e. P2.6 / 300 = P2 / 200P2 = 4 atmThe new pressure of the gas is 4 atm, therefore the answer is C.
As per calculations the answer is 4 atm (refer to the calculation below). Hence, this option is incorrect.
As per calculations the answer is 4 atm (refer to the calculation below). Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The pressure and temperature of an ideal gas are related by the gay-lussac’s law, which states that pressure exerted on a gas varies directly with the absolute temperature of a gas. It is given by the equation: P1 / T1 = P2 / T2Where, P1= 6 atmospheres. T1= 27 ˚C in kelvin: 27+273= 300K T2 = –73 ˚C in kelvin: -73+273= 200K P2 = unknown to be foundSo, we can use this to find the new pressure, i.e. P2.6 / 300 = P2 / 200P2 = 4 atmThe new pressure of the gas is 4 atm, therefore the answer is C.

questions.text: Identify which of the following statements is FALSE.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: The vapor pressure of a liquid decreases with increasing atmospheric pressure
options.explanation: This is a false statement. Vapor pressure is an internal property of a liquid that depends only on the nature of the liquid and its temperature. Changing the external atmospheric pressure does not change the vapor pressure itself; it only changes the boiling point (the temperature at which vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure).

options.text: The value of an equilibrium constant is dependent on temperature
options.explanation: This is a true statement. The equilibrium constant (KC or KP) is a mathematical ratio that changes only when the temperature changes. For exothermic reactions, increasing temperature decreases K, while for endothermic reactions, increasing temperature increases K.

options.text: The rate of a spontaneous reaction cannot be determined solely by its Gibbs free energy
options.explanation: This is a true statement. Gibbs free energy only tells us if a reaction is "spontaneous" (possible), not how fast it will happen. A reaction can be highly spontaneous but still occur at a rate of zero if the activation energy is too high, such as the conversion of diamond to graphite.

options.text: During a phase transition, the temperature of a substance must be constant
options.explanation: This is a true statement. During phase transitions like melting or boiling, all the heat energy provided is used to break intermolecular forces rather than increasing the kinetic energy of the particles. As a result, the temperature stays exactly the same until the phase change is complete.

options.text: The addition of a catalyst to a reaction at equilibrium has no net effect on the system.
options.explanation: This statement is true. This is because a catalyst speeds up the forward and reverse reactions equally, so it does not change the equilibrium constant.

questions.explanation: Vapor pressure is created by molecules escaping from the surface of a liquid, which is influenced by temperature and the strength of the liquid's "glue." Pushing down harder with the atmosphere (increasing atmospheric pressure) doesn't stop those molecules from trying to escape; it just means the liquid has to be heated to a higher temperature before it can finally boil. Therefore, vapor pressure does not decrease just because the air above it is heavier.

questions.text: 50 mL of hydrogen diffuses out through a small hole of a vessel, in 20 minutes. The time taken by 40 mL of oxygen to diffuse out is
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This value would be correct if we were comparing equal volumes of both gases (50mL), as 50mL of oxygen takes 80minutes to diffuse. Since we are only measuring 40mL, the time must be adjusted proportionally, making 32 an incorrect calculation for this specific volume.

This is the correct option. Using Graham's Law, oxygen (being 16 times heavier than hydrogen) diffuses 4 times slower. If 50mL of hydrogen takes 20minutes, then 50mL of oxygen would take 80minutes. Reducing the volume to 40mL (which is 4/5 of the original volume) means it takes 4/5 of 80minutes}, which equals 64minutes.

This value represents a misunderstanding of the relationship between mass and speed. It suggests that oxygen, which is much heavier than hydrogen, would diffuse faster (8 minutes) vs 20minutes). In reality, heavier molecules move much more slowly than light ones.

This value does not follow the square root relationship defined by Graham's Law. It might be reached by a simple linear comparison of volumes or masses without taking the square root of the molar masses into account, leading to an incorrect prediction of the diffusion time.

questions.explanation: Graham's Law of Diffusion, which tells us that lighter gases fly much faster than heavier ones. Since the molecular mass of Oxygen (32) is 16 times greater than that of Hydrogen (2), Oxygen moves √16 = 4 times slower. If 50mL of Hydrogen takes 20 minutes, the same amount of Oxygen would need 80 minutes. Because we are only diffusing 40 mL of Oxygen, we take a fraction of that time, resulting in 64 minutes to finish the process.

questions.text: A jar contains a gas and a few drops of water at 'T' Kelvin temperature. The pressure in the jar is 830mm of Hg. The temperature of the jar is reduced by 1%. The vapour pressure of water at two temperatures are 30 and 25 mm of Hg, respectively. Calculate the new pressure in the jar?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. When the temperature is reduced by 1%, the vapor pressure of water decreases, leading to a new total pressure of 817 mm of Hg.
This option is incorrect because a reduction of pressure due to a temperature decrease does not lead to such a low pressure value.
This option is incorrect as it suggests that the pressure remains unchanged, which contradicts the fact that lowering the temperature affects vapor pressure.
This option is also incorrect as it underestimates the pressure change. The new pressure cannot be this low based on the given information.

questions.explanation: The initial pressure of the dry gas is 830 - 30 = 800 mm Hg. When the temperature is reduced by 1% (to 0.99T), the new gas pressure becomes 800 × 0.99 = 792 mm Hg. The new total pressure is the new gas pressure plus the new vapor pressure: 792 + 25 = 817 mm Hg

questions.text: Ammonia gas and HCl gas from the two flasks, at same temperature and pressure were injected simultaneously through pinholes of similar geometry, attached at the two ends of a 1.0 m long glass tube. At what distance from the ammonia end, the first flash of white fume would be observed?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. Ammonia (NH3) has a molar mass of 17g/mol, while Hydrogen Chloride (HCl) has a molar mass of 36.5g/mol. Using Graham's Law, the ratio of their speeds is √36.5 / 17= approx 1.46. This means ammonia travels 1.46 times faster than HCl. In a 100cm tube, ammonia will cover approximately 59.43cm before meeting the HCl gas.

This value (52.12cm) would be the meeting point if the molar masses of the two gases were much closer to each other. If the gases moved at almost the same speed, they would meet near the center (50cm), but since ammonia is significantly lighter, it must travel much further than the halfway mark.

This distance (34.37cm) represents the distance from the HCl end, not the ammonia end. Since HCl is the heavier and slower gas, it covers less than half of the tube (100 - 59.43 = 40.57cm—actually, 100 / (1 + 1.46)= approx 40.6cm). This option is a common trap for students who calculate the distance for the wrong gas.

This value (49.23cm) suggests that the gases meet slightly before the midpoint. This would only happen if ammonia were heavier than HCl. Since ammonia is actually the lighter gas, it is impossible for it to travel less distance than the HCl in the same amount of time.

questions.explanation: Two gases diffuse from opposite ends of a tube to form white fumes of ammonium chloride (NH4Cl). Because ammonia molecules are lighter (17amu) than HCl molecules (36.5amu), they zip through the air much faster. According to Graham's Law, the lighter gas travels a greater distance in the same amount of time. By calculating the speed ratio, we find that for every centimeter HCl travels, ammonia travels about 1.46cm. This pushes the meeting point past the center of the tube, specifically to 59.43cm from the ammonia side.

questions.text: Gaseous benzene reacts with hydrogen gas in presence of a nickel catalyst to form gaseous cyclohexane according to the reaction: C6H6(g)+ 3H2(g)→ C6H12(g) . A mixture of C6H6 and excess H2 has a pressure of 60 mm of Hg in an unknown volume. After the gas has been passed over a nickel catalyst and all the benzene converted to cyclohexane, the pressure of the gas was 30 mm of Hg in the same volume and temperature. The fraction of C6H6 (by volume) present in the original volume is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option assumes a different stoichiometric relationship between the reactants and products.
This option miscalculates the stoichiometric conversion of benzene to cyclohexane.
This option does not accurately account for the changes in pressure due to the reaction.
This option correctly accounts for the change in total pressure and the stoichiometry of the reaction.

questions.explanation: Following is the solution:
hints: pressure of the gas was 30 mm of Hg

questions.text: 0.2 g of a gas X occupies a volume of 0.44 L at same pressure and temperature. Under identical conditions of P and T, 0.1 g of CO2 gas occupies 0.32 L. Gas X can be :
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: O2 has a molar mass of approximately 32 g/mol. Given the density calculations, it would occupy a larger volume than 0.44 L if 0.2 g were present under the same conditions of pressure and temperature. Therefore, it is not the correct answer.
SO2 has a molar mass of about 64 g/mol. The calculated density of gas X suggests a molar mass close to 64 g/mol, making it a good match for SO2. Hence, this is the correct answer.
NO has a molar mass of approximately 30 g/mol. This would result in a density that does not match the calculated density for gas X. Therefore, it cannot be the correct answer.
C4H10 (butane) has a molar mass around 58 g/mol. The density of this gas would not correspond to the density of gas X based on the given information, making it an incorrect choice.

questions.explanation: According to Avogadro's Law, at the same P and T, moles are proportional to volume (n ∝ V). We can set up a ratio: (moles of X / moles of CO₂) = (V of X / V of CO₂). (0.2/Mₓ) / (0.1/44) = 0.44 / 0.32. Solving for the molar mass of X (Mₓ) gives approximately 64 g/mol, which corresponds to SO₂.
hints: identical conditions of P and T

questions.text: A balloon filled with ethyne is pricked with a sharp, pointed needle and quickly dropped in a tank of H2 gas under identical conditions. After a while, the balloon will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Shrinking would only happen if the gas inside the balloon (Ethyne) escaped faster than the outside gas (Hydrogen) entered. Since Ethyne is much heavier and slower than Hydrogen, this is not possible.

This is the correct choice. According to Graham's Law, lighter gases diffuse faster. Hydrogen (M = 2) is much lighter than Ethyne (C2H2, M = 26). Therefore, Hydrogen molecules will rush into the balloon through the pinhole much faster than Ethyne molecules can leak out, causing the balloon to expand.

A complete collapse would require all the gas to leave the balloon without any gas entering. Because the tank is filled with Hydrogen gas, there is an external pressure that will force Hydrogen into the balloon as soon as it is pricked.

The size would only remain unchanged if both gases had the same molar mass and diffused at the same rate. Since there is a large difference in their molecular weights, a net movement of gas molecules will occur, changing the volume.

questions.explanation: The rate of diffusion is inversely proportional to the square root of the molar mass. Ethyne has a molar mass of 26g/mol, while Hydrogen is only 2g/mol. Because Hydrogen is so light, its molecules move with a much higher velocity. When the balloon is pricked, the "zippy" Hydrogen molecules from the tank enter the balloon more quickly than the "heavy" Ethyne molecules can exit. This leads to an increase in the total number of gas particles inside the balloon, which makes it enlarge.

questions.text: Which of the following proportionalities best describes the relationship between the number of intermolecular forces and heat of vaporization for a given substance?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The greater the number of intermolecular forces, the more energy (heat of vaporization) is needed to overcome these forces and vaporize the substance.
If they were inversely proportional, more intermolecular forces would mean less heat of vaporization, which is not the case.
The relationship is consistent; more intermolecular forces lead to a higher heat of vaporization.

options.text: There is no relationship between them
options.explanation: There is a clear relationship; more intermolecular forces result in a higher heat of vaporization.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the number of intermolecular forces and the heat of vaporization are proportional. Intermolecular forces are the attractions between molecules. In a liquid, these forces must be overcome for the molecules to escape into the gas phase, a process requiring energy known as the heat of vaporization. Thus, the more intermolecular forces present, the more energy is needed to vaporize the liquid, indicating a proportional relationship. The other options are incorrect because they either suggest the opposite relationship, deny a relationship, or claim the relationship is too variable to generalize, none of which align with the established understanding of these concepts.

questions.text: Which of the following molecules is likely to have the highest melting point?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is the correct choice. Molecule A shows a linear structure with double bonds on both sides (R=X=R), which allows the molecules to pack very closely together in a crystal lattice. This symmetrical and rigid shape maximizes the surface area for intermolecular attractions, leading to a much higher melting point.

This molecule has a bent or "V-shaped" structure. While bent molecules can have dipole moments, their irregular shape makes it harder for them to stack neatly and tightly in a solid grid compared to linear molecules. Poor packing usually results in a lower melting point.

This is a simple diatomic-like molecule (X-R). Because it is small and has fewer atoms than the others, it generally has a smaller electron cloud and fewer sites for intermolecular forces to act. Small, simple molecules typically have the lowest melting and boiling points in a series.

This molecule has a branched or "Y-shaped" geometry. Branching usually interferes with the ability of molecules to come close to one another. Like a tangled pile of branches compared to neatly stacked logs, these molecules don't fit together well, which weakens the overall solid structure.

questions.explanation: The melting point depends on how strongly molecules are held in a crystal lattice. Two main factors are at play here: the strength of intermolecular forces and how well the molecules "fit" together (packing efficiency). Symmetrical, linear molecules like the one in Option A can pack very tightly, similar to how bricks are stacked. The closer they are, the harder it is for heat to break them apart and turn the solid into a liquid, resulting in a higher melting point.

questions.text: Which of the following physical conditions favors a gaseous state for most substances?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: High temperature favors the gas phase, but high pressure works against it. When you apply high pressure, you force molecules closer together, which encourages them to stick and form a liquid or solid. Therefore, this combination is not the most ideal for maintaining a gaseous state.

Low pressure helps molecules stay apart, but low temperature removes their kinetic energy. When molecules move slowly at low temperatures, they are easily captured by attractive forces and tend to settle into a liquid or solid state.

This is the worst combination for a gas. High pressure squeezes molecules together and low temperature makes them move slowly. This specific condition is actually used to liquefy gases, as it maximizes the chances of molecules bonding together.

This is the correct choice. High temperature gives molecules plenty of kinetic energy to move rapidly and break free from each other. Low pressure ensures there is lots of space between them and fewer collisions, preventing them from clustering into a liquid.

questions.explanation: The state of a substance depends on the competition between kinetic energy and intermolecular forces. High temperature increases the kinetic energy of the particles, allowing them to overcome the "sticky" forces holding them together. Meanwhile, low pressure allows the particles to spread out over a large volume. Together, these conditions ensure that the particles remain far apart and in constant, rapid motion, which defines the gaseous state.

questions.text: Which of the following situations would most favor the change of water from a liquid to a solid?I. Decreased solute concentration of a substanceII. Decreased temperature of a substanceIII. Decreased pressure on a substance
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Decreasing the solute concentration (making the water purer) favors freezing. Adding solutes like salt lowers the freezing point of water (freezing point depression), so removing them makes it easier for water molecules to arrange themselves into a solid ice lattice at 0°C.

Decreasing the temperature is the most direct way to favor the solid state. As the temperature drops, water molecules lose kinetic energy and move more slowly, eventually allowing hydrogen bonds to lock them into a fixed, hexagonal crystalline structure.

This is the correct choice because both the removal of impurities (I) and the removal of heat (II) work together to encourage the formation of ice. Lowering the solute concentration raises the freezing point back to normal, and lowering the temperature provides the thermal condition needed for freezing.

Decreasing the pressure on water actually favors the liquid state or prevents freezing. Water is unique because ice is less dense than liquid water; therefore, increasing pressure actually helps melt ice into liquid, while decreasing pressure (at a constant temperature) does not specifically push it toward the solid state like it does for most other substances.

questions.explanation: Freezing is a process where molecules slow down enough to form a rigid structure. Two things usually get in the way of this: heat and impurities. High temperature keeps molecules moving too fast to bond, and solutes (like salt) physically block the water molecules from joining the ice crystal. By decreasing the temperature and decreasing the solute concentration, you remove both obstacles, making it much easier for water to turn into a solid.

questions.text: The heats of vaporization of four substances are given in the below. Which of these substances has the lowest boiling point?Comparative Heats of Vaporization Liquid Heat Required (cal/g)Chlorine 67.4Ether 9.4Carbon dioxide 72.2Ammonia 295
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: All of the other answer choices have higher heats of vaporization, as seen in the table associated with the question.
(B) has the lowest heat required for vaporization, which by definition determines the boiling point or the lowest temperature required for phase change from liquid to gas.
All of the other answer choices have higher heats of vaporization, as seen in the table associated with the question.
All of the other answer choices have higher heats of vaporization, as seen in the table associated with the question.

questions.explanation: The boiling point of a liquid is the temperature at which its vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure. This process depends on overcoming intermolecular forces. The heat of vaporization measures the energy needed to break these forces. There is a direct relationship: the weaker the intermolecular forces, the lower the heat of vaporization, and the lower the boiling point. Since ether requires the least amount of heat (9.4cal/g) to vaporize, it will reach its boiling point much faster and at a lower temperature than the others.

questions.text: Alloys are mixtures of new metals in either the liquid or solid phase. Which of the following is usually true of alloys?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: The melting/freezing point of an alloy will be lower than that of either of the component metals because the new bonds are stronger
options.explanation: While it is true that the melting point of an alloy is usually lower than its parent metals, the reasoning here is incorrect. If the new bonds were stronger, it would require more energy (higher temperature) to break them, which would increase the melting point rather than lowering it.

options.text: The melting/freezing point of an alloy will be lower than that of either of the component metals because the new bonds are weaker
options.explanation: This is the correct choice. When a different metal is added to a pure metal, the foreign atoms disrupt the regular, neat arrangement of the crystal lattice. This interference makes the overall metallic bonding "messier" and effectively weaker, allowing the solid to melt into a liquid at a lower temperature.

options.text: The melting/freezing point of an alloy will be greater than that of either of the component metals because the new bonds are weaker
options.explanation: This option is contradictory. If bonds are weaker, they are easier to break, which always leads to a lower melting point, not a greater one. In chemistry, weaker attractions between particles always result in a more easily disrupted solid structure.

options.text: The melting/freezing point of an alloy will be greater than that of either of the component metals because the new bonds are stronger
options.explanation: This is generally incorrect for simple alloys. While some specific "intermetallic compounds" can have high melting points, the vast majority of common alloys (like solder or brass) are designed or naturally occur to have melting points lower than at least one of their constituent pure metals due to lattice distortion.

questions.explanation: Pure metals have a very stable and uniform crystalline structure. An alloy is essentially a solid solution where "impurity" atoms are mixed in. These different-sized atoms prevent the metal atoms from packing together perfectly. Because the atoms cannot get as close or stay as organized, the attractive forces holding the solid together are easier to overcome. This is why a mixture of lead and tin (solder) melts at a much lower temperature than either pure lead or pure tin.

questions.text: Viscosity of a liquid is high if
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: The molecules are large and elongated
options.explanation: Large and elongated (long) molecules tend to get tangled with one another as they try to move. This physical entanglement, combined with greater surface area for intermolecular attractions, creates significant internal friction, which results in high viscosity.

options.text: The molecules are small and spherical
options.explanation: Small, spherical molecules act like tiny marbles; they can roll and slide past each other very easily with minimal friction. Substances with these types of molecules usually have very low viscosity and flow quite quickly.

options.text: The temperature of the liquid is increased
options.explanation: Increasing the temperature actually decreases viscosity. When a liquid is heated, the molecules gain kinetic energy and move faster, which helps them overcome the attractive forces holding them back, making the liquid "thinner" and more fluid.

Weak intermolecular forces mean the molecules aren't "sticky" towards each other. If the forces are weak, the molecules can slide past each other with almost no resistance, leading to low viscosity rather than high.

questions.explanation: Viscosity is defined as the internal resistance of a liquid to flow. Think of it like "thickness." For a liquid to have high viscosity, its molecules must find it difficult to move past their neighbors. This happens most effectively when molecules are long and irregular in shape because they wrap around each other like tangled pieces of string. Additionally, stronger intermolecular forces like hydrogen bonding act as a "molecular glue" that further prevents the liquid from flowing freely.

questions.text: When substance moves from a solid to a liquid state all of the following changes occur except
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is correct. The transition from a solid to a liquid involves molecules moving from an ordered lattice structure to a more random and fluid arrangement.
This is incorrect. During melting, the kinetic energy of the molecules actually increases as they absorb heat and gain energy to overcome the rigid structure of the solid state.
This is correct. As a substance melts, the intermolecular forces that hold the molecules in a fixed position become weaker, allowing the molecules to move more freely.
This is correct. In the liquid state, molecules are more spaced out compared to the tightly packed structure of a solid.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the kinetic energy (K.E) of the molecules decreases. During the transition from solid to liquid, energy is absorbed as heat, causing an increase in the kinetic energy of the molecules. This energy allows them to overcome the strong intermolecular forces present in the solid state, causing them to move more freely and become more disordered in the liquid form. The other options correctly describe changes that occur during this phase transition: molecules become more disordered, intermolecular forces weaken, and molecules become more separated.

questions.text: What is the total number of Bravis lattices in the crystal structures?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because there are more than 3 types of Bravais lattices.
This option is incorrect because there are more than 6 types of Bravais lattices.
This is the correct option. There are 14 distinct Bravais lattices that represent the different possible symmetries in three-dimensional space.
This option is incorrect. The total number of Bravais lattices is 14, not 24.

questions.explanation: Crystalline solids are categorized based on their symmetry. There are 7 basic shapes or "crystal systems" (like cubic, tetragonal, etc.). When we consider where atoms can be placed, not just at the corners but also in the center or on the faces of these shapes, we get different variations. Although many combinations are mathematically possible, only 14 of them are unique and do not overlap in symmetry. These are known as the 14 Bravais lattices.

questions.text: _ is a crystalline’s basic repetitive structural unit.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A monomer is a single molecule that can join with other monomers to form a polymer. It is not related to crystal structure.
This option is incorrect. Molecules can be part of a crystal, but they do not make up the repeating structural unit in a crystalline solid.
This option is correct. The unit cell is the basic repetitive structural unit in a crystalline solid, defining the crystal's symmetry and structure.
This option is incorrect. While atoms are the building blocks of a crystal, the unit cell is the smallest repeating unit that defines the crystal structure, not a single atom.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the 'Unit cell,' which is the fundamental building block of a crystal lattice. It is the smallest repeating unit that, when stacked in three-dimensional space, creates the entire crystal structure. The unit cell captures the symmetry and arrangement of the constituent atoms, ions, or molecules in the crystal. Other options like 'Monomer,' 'Molecule,' and 'Atom' do not specifically refer to the repeating structural unit of a crystal. A 'Monomer' is related to polymers, a 'Molecule' can be part of a crystal's composition but not its repetitive unit, and an 'Atom' is part of the substance but does not define the crystal's repeating pattern.

questions.text: A crystalline solid:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: Changes abruptly from solid to liquid when heated
options.explanation: This is correct for certain crystalline solids. They possess a distinct and sharp melting point where they transition from solid to liquid, a characteristic of their well-ordered internal arrangement.
This is incorrect. Crystalline solids are known for having a precise melting point due to their ordered structure, unlike amorphous solids, which melt over a range of temperatures.

options.text: Undergoes deformation of its geometry easily
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Crystalline solids have a rigid structure with atoms fixed in a regular pattern, making them resistant to deformation.
This is incorrect. Crystalline solids exhibit a highly regular, repeating 3-dimensional atomic arrangement, which is the opposite of an irregular structure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that a crystalline solid changes abruptly from solid to liquid when heated. This is due to its precise and orderly atomic arrangement, leading to a specific melting point. Option A is correct because it highlights this distinct property. Option B is incorrect because crystalline solids do have a definite melting point. Option C is incorrect, as crystalline solids are resistant to deformation due to their rigid structure. Option D is incorrect because it describes amorphous solids, not crystalline ones.

questions.text: For the orthorhombic system, axial ratios are a ≠ b ≠ c and the axial angles are:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the orthorhombic system has all three angles equal to 90 degrees, not different from 90 degrees.
This option is incorrect as it suggests none of the angles are 90 degrees, which does not align with the orthorhombic system where all angles are 90 degrees.
This is the correct option because the orthorhombic crystal system features three perpendicular axes, meaning all angles are 90 degrees.
This option is incorrect as it implies two angles differ and one is 90 degrees, which is not the case in the orthorhombic system where all three angles are exactly 90 degrees.

questions.explanation: The orthorhombic crystal system is characterized by having three axes of different lengths (a ≠ b ≠ c), but all three axes are perpendicular to each other. Therefore, the angles between the axes are all 90 degrees (α = β = γ = 90o). Option C is correct because it accurately describes the orthorhombic system. Options A, B, and D suggest variations in the angles or incorrect angles, which do not fit the orthorhombic crystal structure.

questions.text: Which type of solids will have the highest melting points?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Ionic solids are composed of ions held together by strong electrostatic forces, resulting in high melting points. However, they typically do not surpass the melting points of network covalent solids.

Network covalent solids consist of atoms interconnected by covalent bonds in a continuous network throughout the material. This extensive bonding network gives them exceptionally high melting points, often higher than other types of solids.

Molecular solids are made of discrete molecules bound by weak intermolecular forces such as van der Waals forces or hydrogen bonds. Consequently, they have lower melting points compared to ionic and covalent network solids.

Metallic crystals consist of metal atoms held together by metallic bonds, which allow electrons to move freely. They have relatively high melting points, but generally not as high as network covalent solids.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Network covalent solids. These solids are held together by an extensive network of covalent bonds, which confer structural stability and result in very high melting points, often exceeding those of other solid types.Ionic solids have strong electrostatic forces between ions, leading to high melting points, yet they are typically not as high as network covalent solids. Molecular solids have much lower melting points due to weak intermolecular forces. Metallic crystals have high melting points due to metallic bonding, but they generally fall short of the melting points observed in network covalent solids.

questions.text: What is the relation between diamond and graphite?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option is correct. Diamond and graphite are polymorphs of carbon, meaning they have the same chemical composition but different crystal structures.

Isomers are molecules with the same molecular formula but different arrangements of atoms. This term does not apply to diamond and graphite as they are forms of a single element, carbon, not compounds.
Isotopes refer to atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons. Diamond and graphite are not isotopes; they are different forms of carbon atoms.
Isomorphous refers to different compounds with the same crystal structure. Diamond and graphite do not share the same structure; hence, this term is not applicable.

questions.explanation: Diamond and graphite are both allotropes of carbon, which means they are polymorphous. They share the same chemical composition, carbon, but differ in their crystal structures. Diamond has a tetrahedral lattice structure, making it extremely hard, while graphite has a planar layered structure, allowing it to be soft and slippery. This structural difference is why option A, 'Polymorphous,' is correct. Options B, C, and D are incorrect because they describe different relationships: 'Isomer' applies to compounds, 'Isotope' applies to elements with different neutron counts, and 'Isomorphous' refers to different compounds with similar crystal structures.

questions.text: In crystalline solids, which of the following element of symmetry is not present?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: An axis of symmetry is a line about which a crystal can be rotated by a certain angle and appear unchanged. This is a common element of symmetry found in many crystalline solids.
The term 'angle of symmetry' is not a standard element of symmetry in crystalline solids. Crystalline symmetry elements typically refer to axes, planes, and centers, not angles.

A center of symmetry is a point within a crystal where each atom has an equivalent atom located diametrically opposite at the same distance from the center. This is a recognized element of symmetry in crystalline solids.
A plane of symmetry divides a crystal into two mirror-image halves. This is a common symmetry element in crystalline materials.

questions.explanation: The concept of an 'angle of symmetry' does not exist in the study of crystalline solids. Symmetry elements in crystallography typically include the axis of symmetry, plane of symmetry, and center of symmetry, all of which describe specific geometric properties of a crystal. The axis of symmetry allows rotation, the plane of symmetry reflects mirror-image halves, and the center of symmetry involves diametrically opposite points. The 'angle of symmetry' is not a recognized term within this framework, making it the correct answer for the element that is not present in crystalline solids.

questions.text: The most symmetrical crystal system is
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The triclinic crystal system is the least symmetrical among the seven crystal systems. It has no perpendicular axes and no equal angles.
The tetragonal crystal system has a fourfold axis of symmetry and is less symmetrical than the cubic system.

The cubic crystal system has the highest degree of symmetry among all the crystal systems. It has four threefold rotation axes, three fourfold rotation axes, and six twofold rotation axes. Moreover, it has four threefold axes that are mutually perpendicular.
The rhombohedral crystal system has a threefold axis of symmetry but is less symmetrical than the cubic system.

questions.explanation: The question pertains to crystal systems, which are a set of categories in which crystals are grouped based on their symmetry properties. Different crystal systems exhibit different degrees of symmetry. The question asks about the most symmetrical crystal system among the options provided. The crystal systems listed are the seven main crystal systems based on the classification by crystallographers.These crystal systems are as follows:1. Triclinic2. Tetragonal3. Cubic4. RhombohedralAmong these options, the cubic crystal system is known for its high degree of symmetry. It has multiple rotational axes and perpendicular axes, making it highly symmetrical. Therefore, the correct answer to the question is c. Cubic.

questions.text: The molecules of CO2 in dry ice form
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Covalent crystals are formed when atoms are held together by covalent bonds. Dry ice, consisting of CO2 molecules, does not involve covalent bonding between its molecules, so it does not form covalent crystals.
Ionic crystals are composed of positively and negatively charged ions held together by electrostatic forces. Since dry ice is made up of neutral CO2 molecules, it cannot form ionic crystals.
Metallic crystals consist of metal atoms bonded together by delocalized electrons. Dry ice contains no metal atoms, so it does not form metallic crystals.

Molecular crystals consist of molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces, such as van der Waals forces or hydrogen bonds. Dry ice is made up of CO2 molecules held together by van der Waals forces, making it a molecular crystal.

questions.explanation: Dry ice is the solid form of carbon dioxide (CO2), and its crystalline structure is formed by CO2 molecules held together by weak van der Waals forces. These characteristics classify it as a molecular crystal. The other options are incorrect because:Covalent crystals: Formed by covalent bonds between atoms, which is not the case for CO2 molecules in dry ice.Ionic crystals: Composed of charged ions, but dry ice consists of neutral CO2 molecules.Metallic crystals: Composed of metal atoms with metallic bonding, which is not applicable to dry ice.

questions.text: When the atoms of the third layer are arranged in such a way that they directly lie above the atoms of the first layer then this arrangement is called
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: In this arrangement, the third layer directly lies above the first layer. This arrangement is characteristic of the hexagonal close-packed (HCP) crystal structure.
This arrangement suggests a face-centered cubic (FCC) structure, where the stacking sequence of the layers is such that each layer directly lies above the other in a repeating pattern.
Orthorhombic refers to a crystal system characterized by three mutually perpendicular axes of different lengths.
Rhombohedral refers to a crystal system that is closely related to the hexagonal system, but with oblique angles.

questions.explanation: The question pertains to the arrangement of atoms in crystal structures, particularly focusing on the arrangement of layers. Some crystal structures have a specific pattern in which the layers of atoms are arranged.The question specifically mentions the arrangement of the third layer in relation to the first layer. It asks about the specific configuration when the atoms of the third layer are positioned directly above the atoms of the first layer.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements about packing in solids is incorrect?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: Coordination number in bcc mode of packing is 8
options.explanation: This statement is correct. In body-centred cubic (bcc) packing, each atom is surrounded by 8 neighbouring atoms, resulting in a coordination number of 8.

options.text: Coordination number in hcp mode of packing is 12
options.explanation: This statement is correct. In hexagonal close-packed (hcp) packing, each atom is surrounded by 12 neighbouring atoms, giving it a coordination number of 12.

options.text: Void space in hcp mode of packing is 32%
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The void space in hexagonal close-packed (hcp) packing is actually about 26%, not 32%.

options.text: Void space in ccp mode of packing is 26%
options.explanation: This statement is correct. In cubic close-packed (ccp) or face-centered cubic (fcc) packing, the void space is indeed about 26%.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: 'Void space in hcp mode of packing is 32%.' This statement is incorrect because the void space in hexagonal close-packed (hcp) packing is about 26%, not 32%. The other options are correct: Option A correctly states that the coordination number in bcc packing is 8, Option B correctly states that the coordination number in hcp packing is 12, and Option D correctly states that the void space in ccp packing is 26%.

questions.text: Among the following, the incorrect statement is
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: The density of crystals remains unaffected due to the Frenkel defect
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Frenkel defects involve the displacement of ions within the crystal lattice without changing the overall number of ions, so the density remains unchanged.

options.text: In a BCC unit cell, the void space is approximately 32%
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The body-centered cubic (BCC) unit cell has a void space of about 32% due to the arrangement of atoms.

options.text: The density of crystals decreases due to the Schottky defect
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Schottky defects cause vacancies in the crystal lattice, reducing the density as both cations and anions are missing.

options.text: The electrical conductivity of semiconductors and metals increases with an increase in temperature
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. For semiconductors, conductivity typically increases with temperature as more charge carriers are available. However, for metals, conductivity decreases because increased temperature causes more resistance.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D. The statement is incorrect because, in metals, electrical conductivity decreases with temperature due to increased resistance. In contrast, semiconductors show increased conductivity with temperature as more charge carriers are generated. Options A, B, and C are correct statements about Frenkel defects, BCC unit cells, and Schottky defects, respectively, as they correctly describe how these defects and structures affect density and void space.

questions.text: What are not key factors of solid-state reactions?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is a key factor. The size of particles affects the surface area available for reaction, influencing the reactivity and rate of solid-state reactions.
Mechanical properties are significant in solid-state reactions. The crystal structure and any defects can affect how the reaction proceeds.
These factors are crucial. Thermodynamics determines the feasibility of a reaction, while kinetics dictates the speed at which it occurs.
These are not typically considered key factors in solid-state reactions. While temperature and pressure influence chemical reactions, they are not primary factors in solid-state chemistry.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D, "Environmental Factors." In solid-state reactions, factors like the size of particles, mechanical properties, and both thermodynamic and kinetic factors are crucial. These determine the reaction's feasibility, speed, and mechanism. Although environmental conditions such as temperature and pressure do affect chemical reactions, they are not primary considerations in the context of solid-state reactions.

questions.text: A cubic crystal has
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: Thirteen planes of symmetry and nine axes of symmetry
options.explanation: This option incorrectly swaps the counts. While a cubic crystal has 13 axes and 9 planes, stating that it has 13 planes and 9 axes is a common point of confusion. Remember that there are more axes because they can pass through faces, edges, or corners, while planes are more restricted.

options.text: Thirteen axes of symmetry and nine planes of symmetry
options.explanation: This is the correct choice. A cubic crystal possesses 13 axes of symmetry (3 axes of 4-fold, 4 axes of 3-fold, and 6 axes of 2-fold symmetry) and 9 planes of symmetry (3 rectangular and 6 diagonal). Together with 1 center of symmetry, these make up the 23 total elements of symmetry.

options.text: Thirteen axes and thirteen planes of symmetry
options.explanation: While it is tempting to think the numbers are equal because the cube is so balanced, it does not have 13 planes. The geometry of a cube only allows for 3 planes that cut through the faces and 6 that cut diagonally through the edges, totaling 9.

options.text: Nine planes and seven axes of symmetry
options.explanation: This option correctly identifies the 9 planes but underestimates the number of axes. A cube has far more than 7 axes. By looking at all possible points of rotation (corners to corners, mid-edges to mid-edges, and face-centers to face-centers), you find exactly 13.
